title: CrewAI Research Team (5-Agent Crew with Hierarchical Process)
tldr: 5-agent CrewAI crew: researcher -> analyst -> fact-checker -> writer -> editor. Task delegation, hierarchical process, role-based prompts.
description: Role-driven multi-agent crew with declarative task graph: each agent has a goal, backstory, allowed tools; tasks define expected_output + dependencies.
when_to_use: Linear-ish workflows (research -> analyze -> write -> review) where role definitions and declarative task ordering are clearer than LangGraph state machines. CrewAI shines for content generation, market research, and compliance-style sequential review.
when_not_to_use: Skip CrewAI when (1) you need dynamic branching that depends on intermediate output - LangGraph wins; (2) latency matters - CrewAI's sequential default adds round-trips; (3) you need fine-grained state control - CrewAI hides too much.
quick_start: 1) `pip install crewai crewai-tools`  2) Define agents + tasks + crew in one file  3) `python crew.py 'OSS local LLM serving in 2026'`
full_code:
"""CrewAI 5-agent research crew."""
from __future__ import annotations

import os

from crewai import Agent, Crew, Process, Task
from crewai_tools import SerperDevTool, WebsiteSearchTool


def build_crew(topic: str) -> Crew:
    search = SerperDevTool()
    web = WebsiteSearchTool()

    researcher = Agent(
        role="Senior Research Analyst",
        goal=f"Find authoritative sources about: {topic}",
        backstory="You spent 10 years at a top consultancy. You only cite primary sources.",
        tools=[search, web],
        llm="claude-sonnet-4-5",
        verbose=True,
    )
    analyst = Agent(
        role="Data Analyst",
        goal="Extract quantitative claims + benchmarks from research notes",
        backstory="You are skeptical of round numbers and demand source citations for every claim.",
        llm="claude-sonnet-4-5",
        verbose=True,
    )
    fact_checker = Agent(
        role="Fact-Checker",
        goal="Verify each claim against the cited source; flag anything unverifiable",
        backstory="You spot bullshit for a living. Your only output: 'verified', 'unverifiable', or 'contradicted'.",
        tools=[search],
        llm="claude-sonnet-4-5",
        verbose=True,
    )
    writer = Agent(
        role="Technical Writer",
        goal="Write a 600-word brief from verified findings only",
        backstory="You write for engineers. No fluff. Concrete claims with inline citations.",
        llm="claude-sonnet-4-5",
        verbose=True,
    )
    editor = Agent(
        role="Senior Editor",
        goal="Cut 30%, sharpen prose, ensure each claim has a citation",
        backstory="You hate adjectives and run-on sentences.",
        llm="claude-sonnet-4-5",
        verbose=True,
    )

    research_task = Task(description=f"Research: {topic}. Output 5-10 key findings with primary sources.", agent=researcher, expected_output="Bullet list of findings with sources.")
    analyze_task = Task(description="Extract every quantitative claim. Tag each with a source.", agent=analyst, expected_output="Table: claim | value | source URL.", context=[research_task])
    factcheck_task = Task(description="Verify each claim from the analyst.", agent=fact_checker, expected_output="Annotated table with 'verified'/'unverifiable'/'contradicted' per row.", context=[analyze_task])
    write_task = Task(description="Write 600-word brief using only verified claims.", agent=writer, expected_output="600-word markdown brief with inline [n] citations.", context=[factcheck_task])
    edit_task = Task(description="Cut 30%, tighten prose, verify citations.", agent=editor, expected_output="Final 420-word brief.", context=[write_task])

    return Crew(
        agents=[researcher, analyst, fact_checker, writer, editor],
        tasks=[research_task, analyze_task, factcheck_task, write_task, edit_task],
        process=Process.sequential,
        verbose=True,
    )


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import sys
    topic = sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 else "OSS local LLM serving in 2026"
    crew = build_crew(topic)
    result = crew.kickoff()
    print(result.raw)

production_checklist: Always set llm= per agent explicitly - mixing models silently degrades quality.

Cap max_execution_time on the Crew - hung tools will eat your API budget.

Pin crewai minor version - 0.85 -> 0.86 changed Agent signature and broke many examples.
For repeated workflows, cache task outputs by inputs hash + dependency hash.
Eval against single-agent baseline before shipping - CrewAI overhead is real (3-5x cost).

faq.question: CrewAI vs LangGraph - when each?
faq.answer: CrewAI: declarative, role-first, best for linear content workflows (research -> draft -> edit). LangGraph: imperative, state-first, best for dynamic branching workflows (router agents, conditional retrieval). If your workflow fits on a flowchart with straight arrows, CrewAI is faster to write.

faq.question: Can I use local LLMs with CrewAI?
faq.answer: Yes - pass `llm='ollama/llama3.3:70b'` or any LiteLLM provider string. Quality degrades on smaller models (the role-playing depends on instruction-following capability that 70B has, 8B struggles with).

title: AutoGen Conversable Agents with Code Execution
tldr: Microsoft AutoGen conversable agents with Python code-exec sandbox, user-proxy approval gates, group-chat orchestration.
description: AutoGen v0.4 agentic chat: ConversableAgent + UserProxyAgent + AssistantAgent in a group chat where one agent writes code, another executes it in a Docker sandbox.
when_to_use: Tasks where agents need to write + execute code iteratively: data analysis, plotting, scientific computing, debugging. AutoGen's Docker-backed code execution is the most production-ready sandbox in OSS multi-agent.
when_not_to_use: Skip AutoGen when (1) no code execution needed - LangGraph/CrewAI are simpler; (2) you need declarative task graphs - AutoGen is conversation-first; (3) you can't run Docker - AutoGen's safest exec relies on containers.
quick_start: 1) `pip install autogen-agentchat autogen-ext[docker]`  2) Docker running locally  3) `python autogen_code.py 'Plot Llama 3.3 70B vs GPT-5 tok/s'`
full_code:
"""AutoGen 0.4 group chat with code execution."""
from __future__ import annotations

import asyncio
import os

from autogen_agentchat.agents import AssistantAgent, UserProxyAgent
from autogen_agentchat.teams import RoundRobinGroupChat
from autogen_agentchat.conditions import TextMentionTermination, MaxMessageTermination
from autogen_ext.models.anthropic import AnthropicChatCompletionClient
from autogen_ext.tools.code_execution import PythonCodeExecutionTool
from autogen_ext.code_executors.docker import DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor


async def main(task: str) -> None:
    model = AnthropicChatCompletionClient(model="claude-sonnet-4-5", api_key=os.environ["ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"])

    # Code executor: Docker sandbox, 30s timeout per cell
    code_executor = DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor(work_dir="./autogen_work", timeout=30, image="python:3.11-slim")
    await code_executor.start()
    code_tool = PythonCodeExecutionTool(code_executor)

    planner = AssistantAgent(
        name="planner",
        model_client=model,
        system_message="You are a data analyst. Plan how to answer the user's question, then ask the coder to execute Python. Say TERMINATE when done.",
    )
    coder = AssistantAgent(
        name="coder",
        model_client=model,
        tools=[code_tool],
        system_message="You write Python. Always print results. Use matplotlib for plots; save to ./out/ then describe the plot.",
    )

    termination = TextMentionTermination("TERMINATE") | MaxMessageTermination(10)
    team = RoundRobinGroupChat([planner, coder], termination_condition=termination)
    result = await team.run(task=task)
    for msg in result.messages:
        print(f"\n[{msg.source}] {msg.content}")
    await code_executor.stop()


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import sys
    task = sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 else "Compute fibonacci(30) and print it."
    asyncio.run(main(task))

# AND tell agents in system_message to say TERMINATE when done

production_checklist: ALWAYS use DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor in production - LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor will run any code on your host.
Set MaxMessageTermination as a hard cap (10-20) - infinite loops eat tokens fast.
Bind-mount only the work_dir, never the home directory.
Run as non-root inside the container.
Cap container CPU + memory: `docker_runtime_kwargs={'mem_limit': '1g', 'cpus': 2}`.
Log every code block before execution for audit.
Pin autogen-agentchat to a minor version - v0.4 -> v0.5 will likely break APIs.

faq.question: AutoGen vs LangGraph for code-exec agents?
faq.answer: AutoGen has the most mature code-execution story (Docker sandbox, approval gates, retry-on-error built-in). LangGraph can do code-exec via a tool but you write the sandbox layer yourself. For data analysis / scientific agents, AutoGen is the safer default; for everything else LangGraph is more flexible.

faq.question: Is the code execution actually safe?
faq.answer: With DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor + non-root user + cpu/mem limits + read-only host mounts: yes, safe enough for internal use. NOT safe for untrusted user input - prompt injection can still bias the agent into writing malicious code that runs in the sandbox. Add a code review step (UserProxyAgent input_func) for public-facing apps.

title: OpenAI Swarm Agent Handoff Pattern
tldr: OpenAI Swarm pattern: minimal agent abstraction with function-based handoff between agents. Stateless, easy to reason about.
description: Swarm's clean handoff model: each agent has tools, one tool returns an Agent object to transfer control. Stateless, debuggable, no graph state to manage.
when_to_use: When you want the simplest possible multi-agent pattern with no state management - just functions that return other agents. Perfect for support routing (general -> billing -> tier-2), conversational flows, or any star-topology agent system.
when_not_to_use: Skip Swarm when (1) you need durable state across agent transfers - Swarm is intentionally stateless; (2) you need parallel execution; (3) you need observability + retries built-in - Swarm gives you raw primitives.
quick_start: 1) `pip install git+https://github.com/openai/swarm.git`  2) Define agents + handoff functions  3) `python swarm_app.py`
full_code:
"""OpenAI Swarm handoff pattern - support triage."""
from __future__ import annotations

import os

from swarm import Swarm, Agent


client = Swarm()


def transfer_to_billing():
    return billing_agent


def transfer_to_tech():
    return tech_agent


def transfer_to_general():
    return general_agent


general_agent = Agent(
    name="General Support",
    instructions="You triage support requests. If billing, hand off to billing. If technical, hand off to tech. Otherwise handle yourself.",
    functions=[transfer_to_billing, transfer_to_tech],
)

billing_agent = Agent(
    name="Billing Specialist",
    instructions="You handle billing, refunds, subscription changes. Be precise about dates and amounts.",
    functions=[transfer_to_general],
)

tech_agent = Agent(
    name="Technical Support",
    instructions="You troubleshoot technical issues. Ask for error messages and OS details.",
    functions=[transfer_to_general],
)


def run_loop():
    print("OSS AI Hub Support. Type 'quit' to exit.")
    messages = []
    agent = general_agent
    while True:
        user = input("\nYou: ")
        if user.strip().lower() == "quit":
            return
        messages.append({"role": "user", "content": user})
        response = client.run(agent=agent, messages=messages)
        messages = response.messages
        agent = response.agent
        print(f"\n[{agent.name}] {response.messages[-1]['content']}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    run_loop()

production_checklist: Swarm is officially experimental - pin to a git SHA, never to main.
OpenAI now recommends the `agents` SDK (also from openai) for production - evaluate that before committing to Swarm.
Add structured logging per agent transfer - debugging is harder than LangGraph since there's no central state.
Cap conversation length (last 30 messages) - Swarm has no built-in trimming.
Use context_variables for shared state - don't smuggle state through messages.
Test failure modes (handoff to nonexistent agent) - Swarm doesn't error gracefully.

faq.question: Swarm vs LangGraph vs CrewAI - quick decision?
faq.answer: Swarm: minimal handoff, perfect for support routing + simple linear flows. LangGraph: full state machine, best for complex branching + parallel. CrewAI: declarative role-first, best for content workflows. If you can solve it with handoffs alone, Swarm is the smallest abstraction.

faq.question: Is Swarm production-ready in 2026?
faq.answer: OpenAI labels it 'experimental' and has moved focus to the new `openai-agents` SDK. Swarm still works but won't get new features. For new projects, evaluate openai-agents SDK or LangGraph; only pick Swarm if you've already shipped on it and don't want to migrate.

title: smolagents Code-First Agent (HuggingFace)
tldr: HuggingFace smolagents that writes Python to call tools instead of JSON function calls. 30% fewer tool calls for complex tasks; one-screen install.
description: smolagents CodeAgent: model emits Python code that imports tools as functions, executes in a sandbox, returns results. Cleaner than JSON tool-calling for multi-step problems.
when_to_use: Tasks where the agent needs to chain tool calls + do small computations + handle intermediate results (web search -> parse -> compute -> visualize). Code-first agents shave ~30% off tool calls and naturally handle composition.
when_not_to_use: Skip smolagents when (1) tasks are single-tool lookups - regular ReAct is simpler; (2) you can't allow code execution at all - smolagents is fundamentally a code-exec pattern; (3) you need long-running multi-agent orchestration - LangGraph handles that better.
quick_start: 1) `pip install smolagents`  2) `python smol_agent.py 'How many seconds until April 1 2026?'`

Restrict additional_authorized_imports to keep the sandbox tight.

production_checklist: Always set additional_authorized_imports explicitly - don't trust the default.
Move to E2B or remote sandbox for any user-facing deployment - local executor is dev-only.
Cap max_steps (5-10) - infinite loops eat budget.
Log generated code per step - smolagents emits it; capture it.
Add structured error tracebacks so the agent can self-recover.
For multi-tenant: separate sandbox per user (E2B handles this naturally).
Pin smolagents version - the API was unstable through 0.x and is now 1.x.

faq.question: Code-first agents vs JSON tool-calling - which is better?
faq.answer: For multi-step problems (3+ tool calls): code-first wins consistently by 20-40% on benchmarks. The model can write `result = tool_a(); processed = tool_b(result); return tool_c(processed)` in one block instead of three round-trips. For single-tool calls JSON is fine.

faq.question: Is smolagents safe to run on user input?
faq.answer: Not with the local executor. The default Python interpreter sandboxes calls but a determined attacker can find escapes. For public-facing apps: use E2B (`executor_type='e2b'`) or run smolagents inside a Docker container yourself.

title: MCP SQL Server (Read-Only Postgres Access)
tldr: Model Context Protocol server exposing SELECT-only access to Postgres. Schema introspection, row-limit guard, read-only role enforcement.
description: Production MCP server: list_tables / describe_table / query_select tools backed by an asyncpg connection with a read-only Postgres role and row-limit clamp.
when_to_use: You want Claude Desktop, Cursor, or your own agent to query your Postgres safely. MCP gives one protocol for any client; the read-only Postgres role keeps writes impossible at the database layer (defense in depth on top of the tool whitelist).
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) you need writes from the agent - build a separate write server with explicit per-statement approval; (2) your DB has sensitive columns - add a column allow-list at the SQL layer first; (3) you don't run Postgres - swap to SQLAlchemy + bring your own dialect.
quick_start: 1) `pip install 'mcp[cli]' asyncpg`  2) Create a read-only Postgres role  3) `mcp dev sql_server.py` or wire into Claude Desktop config.
full_code:
"""Read-only Postgres MCP server."""
from __future__ import annotations

import os
import re

import asyncpg
from mcp.server.fastmcp import FastMCP


DSN = os.environ["PG_READONLY_DSN"]  # uses a Postgres role with read-only grants
MAX_ROWS = int(os.environ.get("MCP_MAX_ROWS", "200"))

mcp = FastMCP("readonly-sql")
_pool: asyncpg.Pool | None = None


SAFE_SELECT = re.compile(r"^\s*select\b", re.IGNORECASE | re.DOTALL)


async def _get_pool() -> asyncpg.Pool:
    global _pool
    if _pool is None:
        _pool = await asyncpg.create_pool(DSN, min_size=1, max_size=4)
    return _pool


@mcp.tool()
async def list_tables() -> list[dict]:
    """List user-facing tables (excluding pg_* and information_schema)."""
    pool = await _get_pool()
    async with pool.acquire() as conn:
        rows = await conn.fetch("""
            SELECT table_schema, table_name
            FROM information_schema.tables
            WHERE table_schema NOT IN ('pg_catalog', 'information_schema')
            ORDER BY 1, 2
        """)
    return [dict(r) for r in rows]


@mcp.tool()
async def describe_table(schema: str, table: str) -> list[dict]:
    """Return column name + data type for a given table."""
    pool = await _get_pool()
    async with pool.acquire() as conn:
        rows = await conn.fetch("""
            SELECT column_name, data_type, is_nullable
            FROM information_schema.columns
            WHERE table_schema = $1 AND table_name = $2
            ORDER BY ordinal_position
        """, schema, table)
    if not rows:
        return [{"error": f"no such table: {schema}.{table}"}]
    return [dict(r) for r in rows]


@mcp.tool()
async def query_select(sql: str) -> list[dict] | dict:
    """Run a SELECT query (max 200 rows). Anything other than SELECT is rejected."""
    if not SAFE_SELECT.match(sql):
        return {"error": "Only SELECT queries are allowed."}
    if ";" in sql.strip().rstrip(";"):
        return {"error": "Multiple statements are not allowed."}
    if " limit " not in sql.lower():
        sql = sql.rstrip("; ") + f" LIMIT {MAX_ROWS}"
    pool = await _get_pool()
    try:
        async with pool.acquire() as conn:
            rows = await conn.fetch(sql)
        return [dict(r) for r in rows]
    except (asyncpg.PostgresError, ValueError) as e:
        return {"error": f"{type(e).__name__}: {e}"}


if __name__ == "__main__":
    mcp.run()
code:
from mcp.server.fastmcp import FastMCP
import asyncpg, os, re

mcp = FastMCP('readonly-sql')
SAFE = re.compile(r'^\\s*select\\b', re.I | re.S)

@mcp.tool()
async def query_select(sql: str) -> list:
    if not SAFE.match(sql):
        return [{'error': 'SELECT only'}]
    pool = await asyncpg.create_pool(os.environ['PG_READONLY_DSN'])
    async with pool.acquire() as conn:
        rows = await conn.fetch(sql + ' LIMIT 200')
    return [dict(r) for r in rows]

if __name__ == '__main__':
    mcp.run()

env_vars.description: DSN for a Postgres role with SELECT-only grants. Create with: CREATE ROLE mcp_readonly LOGIN PASSWORD '...'; GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA public TO mcp_readonly; GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA public TO mcp_readonly;

Production: register in your client. For Claude Desktop, add to `claude_desktop_config.json`: `{"mcpServers": {"sql": {"command": "python", "args": ["/abs/path/sql_server.py"], "env": {"PG_READONLY_DSN": "..."}}}}`

production_checklist: ALWAYS use a separate Postgres role with SELECT grants only - never use the app role.
Set `statement_timeout=10s` on the readonly role to kill runaway queries.
Add an audit log: log every query + agent identity + timestamp.
Cap row count via MCP_MAX_ROWS - agents will happily SELECT * FROM huge_table.
Block dangerous extensions (dblink, file_fdw) at the database level.
Use a read replica DSN for the agent - keep agent load off the primary.
Pin mcp-python-sdk version - the protocol is still evolving.

faq.question: Why MCP over direct SQL tool-calling?
faq.answer: MCP is a protocol, not a library. One server works across Claude Desktop, Cursor, Zed, Cline, and your own agent. Direct tool-calling locks you into one client. For internal tools where you'll change agents over time, MCP is worth the slight overhead.

faq.question: Is read-only at the role layer enough security?
faq.answer: It's the most important layer (defense in depth). On top of that: query timeout, row cap, blocking DDL via grant control, blocking dangerous extensions, audit logging. Don't rely on the LLM to 'only run safe SQL' - it will eventually do something surprising.

title: MCP Filesystem Server (Sandboxed, TypeScript)
tldr: TypeScript MCP server with read/write file ops constrained to a single directory tree. Path-traversal defense, size limits.
description: MCP server exposing read_file / write_file / list_directory tools sandboxed to a configured root. Uses path.resolve + realpath checks to defeat ../ traversal.
when_to_use: Agents that need to read/write project files (code generation, document editing, file management). The sandbox root keeps the agent inside one directory; path-traversal defense + size caps make it safe to expose to a non-trivial workspace.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) you can give the agent direct CWD access via shell - if you're already trusting it; (2) you need binary file editing - this server is text-oriented; (3) you need real filesystem watch - this is request-response.

production_checklist: Always sandbox to a SPECIFIC directory, never to /.
Resolve ROOT via realpath at startup to defeat symlink tricks.
Cap MCP_MAX_BYTES - 1 MiB default is too high for chat agents handling untrusted prompts.
Log every write_file call with hash + size for audit.
For multi-user setups, sandbox per user with separate ROOT.
Disable write_file for any externally-facing agent.
Pin the SDK version - the JSON-RPC schema is evolving.

faq.question: Why not just give the agent a shell with `cd /allowed/root`?
faq.answer: Shell access lets the agent do anything subprocess-related (network, install packages, fork bombs). MCP tool exposure is the principle-of-least-privilege answer: only the methods you explicitly expose, with size + path validation per call.

faq.question: Is path.resolve enough to prevent ../ attacks?
faq.answer: Almost - the symlink edge case still bites. realpath() resolves symlinks; checking the result starts-with ROOT is the bulletproof variant. The full_code shows this pattern.

faq.question: Why not use the official modelcontextprotocol/servers GitHub server?

faq.question: Fine-grained PAT vs GitHub App?
faq.answer: PAT: faster to set up, expires (refresh manually). App: proper lifecycle, finer permissions, audit logs. For 1-2 user MCP servers a fine-grained PAT is fine; for >5 users or an org-wide tool, build a GitHub App.

production_checklist: Always have a fallback provider - Tavily has had region-wide outages.
Cap query length (this server uses 256 chars) - long queries waste credits.
Cache aggressively - free tiers cap at 1000-2000/mo.
For compliance use cases, set ALLOWED_DOMAINS - reduces info leak from adversarial prompts.
Pipe stdout to a log file - mistakes in fallback logic show up here.
Rotate keys every 90 days.

faq.question: Why Tavily over Bing/Brave/Google?
faq.answer: Tavily was built for LLM consumption - snippets are extracted and include an aggregated answer field. Bing API was retired. Google's Custom Search has rough quotas + complex billing. Brave is the cheapest pure-search fallback. Tavily primary + Brave fallback covers 99% of use cases.

faq.question: Should I rely on the model's built-in web tool instead?
faq.answer: Anthropic's web tool and OpenAI's web search are great for chat agents using those exact models. MCP server is the right answer when (a) you switch between providers, (b) you want a single audit log across agents, or (c) you need domain restriction the built-in doesn't support.

title: MCP Slack Server (Read + Post with Rate-Limit)
tldr: MCP server for Slack: list channels, read history, post messages, manage threads. OAuth flow + rate-limit handling.
description: Python MCP server using slack_sdk: read-only by default, opt-in to posting with channel allow-list, retry-after handling on rate limits.
when_to_use: Agents that summarize Slack threads, monitor channels, or post status updates. The channel allow-list keeps the agent from accidentally pinging #general.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) you only need notifications - Slack webhooks are simpler; (2) you need real-time event response - Slack Events API + a real server beats MCP polling; (3) you have a strict bot review process - this gives the agent posting privilege.
quick_start: 1) Create Slack app at api.slack.com/apps; install to workspace; grab Bot Token  2) `pip install 'mcp[cli]' slack_sdk`  3) `mcp dev slack_server.py`
code:
from mcp.server.fastmcp import FastMCP
from slack_sdk.web.async_client import AsyncWebClient
import os

mcp = FastMCP('slack')
client = AsyncWebClient(token=os.environ['SLACK_BOT_TOKEN'])

@mcp.tool()
async def list_channels():
    res = await client.conversations_list(types='public_channel')
    return [{'id': c['id'], 'name': c['name']} for c in res['channels']]

if __name__ == '__main__':
    mcp.run()

env_vars.name: SLACK_BOT_TOKEN
env_vars.description: xoxb-... Bot Token from your Slack app.

env_vars.name: SLACK_ALLOW_POST
env_vars.description: Set to 'true' to enable posting. Default: read-only.

env_vars.name: SLACK_ALLOWED_CHANNELS
env_vars.description: Comma-separated channel IDs. If set, post_message only works on these.

setup_steps: Create a Slack app at api.slack.com/apps -> From scratch -> name + workspace.
OAuth & Permissions -> Scopes: channels:read, channels:history, chat:write (if posting), groups:read for private.
Install to workspace; copy the Bot Token (`xoxb-...`).
Dev test: `mcp dev slack_server.py` and try list_channels in the inspector.

production_checklist: Default SLACK_ALLOW_POST=false - require explicit opt-in.
Allow-list posting channels via SLACK_ALLOWED_CHANNELS - never let the agent pick freely.
Honor Slack rate limits (Retry-After header) - missing this gets your app banned.
Log every post_message call with full text + agent identity.
Rotate the Bot Token quarterly via Slack app dashboard.
For multi-workspace deployment, switch to OAuth installation flow.
Pin mcp + slack_sdk versions.

faq.question: Why not just use Slack's incoming webhooks?
faq.answer: Webhooks are one-way + post-only. MCP gives the agent the ability to READ history + thread context, which is what makes 'summarize this channel' or 'pull the engineering standup' work.

faq.question: Bot Token vs User Token vs OAuth installation?
faq.answer: Bot Token: simplest, one workspace, scoped to bot identity - use for internal tools. User Token: posts as a real user - avoid unless you really need it. OAuth installation: multi-workspace SaaS - use when distributing to many teams.

title: Unsloth Llama 3.1 8B LoRA Fine-Tune (2x Faster, Single 24GB GPU)
tldr: Unsloth LoRA fine-tune of Llama 3.1 8B on a custom dataset. 2x faster than vanilla HF, fits on a single 24GB consumer GPU (RTX 4090/3090).
description: Unsloth wraps HF Transformers + custom CUDA kernels to fine-tune Llama 3.1 8B with LoRA on a single 24GB card. Full pipeline: load, train, save merged + GGUF.
when_to_use: You have a single 24GB consumer GPU (4090, 3090, 4080 super) and want to fine-tune Llama 3.1 8B on a custom dataset. Unsloth is the fastest open-weight LoRA stack as of 2026 - 2x faster than vanilla HF, ~30% less VRAM, fully compatible with HF Trainer downstream.
when_not_to_use: Skip Unsloth when (1) you need to fine-tune 70B - move to multi-GPU + Axolotl/LLaMA-Factory; (2) you want full-parameter fine-tune - Unsloth is LoRA-only; (3) you're on Apple Silicon - Unsloth is CUDA-only.
quick_start: 1) Install: `pip install unsloth`  2) Prepare a HF dataset of {instruction, output} pairs  3) `python finetune.py` - 2-4h on a 4090 for ~10k examples.
full_code:
"""Unsloth LoRA fine-tune of Llama 3.1 8B on a single 24GB GPU."""
from __future__ import annotations

import os

from datasets import load_dataset
from unsloth import FastLanguageModel
from trl import SFTTrainer
from transformers import TrainingArguments


MODEL = "unsloth/Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct-bnb-4bit"  # pre-quantized for speed
MAX_SEQ = 4096
OUTPUT_DIR = "./outputs/llama3_1_8b_lora"


def format_example(ex: dict) -> dict:
    """Format into Llama 3.1 chat template."""
    text = (
        f"<|begin_of_text|><|start_header_id|>user<|end_header_id|>\n\n"
        f"{ex['instruction']}<|eot_id|><|start_header_id|>assistant<|end_header_id|>\n\n"
        f"{ex['output']}<|eot_id|>"
    )
    return {"text": text}


def main() -> None:
    # 1. Load model + tokenizer (4-bit)
    model, tokenizer = FastLanguageModel.from_pretrained(
        model_name=MODEL,
        max_seq_length=MAX_SEQ,
        dtype=None,                     # auto: bf16 on Ampere+, fp16 on older
        load_in_4bit=True,
    )

    # 2. Attach LoRA adapters
    model = FastLanguageModel.get_peft_model(
        model,
        r=16,                           # LoRA rank - 16-32 is the sweet spot
        target_modules=["q_proj", "k_proj", "v_proj", "o_proj", "gate_proj", "up_proj", "down_proj"],
        lora_alpha=16,
        lora_dropout=0,
        bias="none",
        use_gradient_checkpointing="unsloth",  # 30% less VRAM
        random_state=42,
    )

    # 3. Load + format dataset (replace with your own)
    raw = load_dataset("yahma/alpaca-cleaned", split="train[:5000]")
    raw = raw.map(lambda ex: format_example({"instruction": ex["instruction"], "output": ex["output"]}))

    # 4. Train
    args = TrainingArguments(
        output_dir=OUTPUT_DIR,
        per_device_train_batch_size=2,
        gradient_accumulation_steps=8,
        warmup_steps=50,
        max_steps=500,
        learning_rate=2e-4,
        bf16=True,
        logging_steps=10,
        save_steps=200,
        save_total_limit=2,
        optim="adamw_8bit",
        weight_decay=0.01,
        lr_scheduler_type="cosine",
        seed=42,
        report_to="none",
    )
    trainer = SFTTrainer(
        model=model,
        tokenizer=tokenizer,
        train_dataset=raw,
        dataset_text_field="text",
        max_seq_length=MAX_SEQ,
        packing=False,
        args=args,
    )
    trainer.train()

    # 5. Save LoRA adapter + merge + export GGUF for llama.cpp
    model.save_pretrained(f"{OUTPUT_DIR}/lora")
    tokenizer.save_pretrained(f"{OUTPUT_DIR}/lora")
    model.save_pretrained_merged(f"{OUTPUT_DIR}/merged", tokenizer, save_method="merged_16bit")
    model.save_pretrained_gguf(f"{OUTPUT_DIR}/gguf", tokenizer, quantization_method="q4_k_m")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
code:
"""Unsloth LoRA fine-tune (single 24GB GPU)."""
from unsloth import FastLanguageModel
from trl import SFTTrainer
from transformers import TrainingArguments
from datasets import load_dataset

model, tok = FastLanguageModel.from_pretrained('unsloth/Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct-bnb-4bit', max_seq_length=4096, load_in_4bit=True)
model = FastLanguageModel.get_peft_model(model, r=16, lora_alpha=16, target_moduleses=['q_proj','k_proj','v_proj','o_proj'], use_gradient_checkpointing='unsloth')

ds = load_dataset('yahma/alpaca-cleaned', split='train[:5000]')
trainer = SFTTrainer(model=model, tokenizer=tok, train_dataset=ds, dataset_text_field='text', max_seq_length=4096,
    args=TrainingArguments(output_dir='./out', per_device_train_batch_size=2, gradient_accumulation_steps=8, max_steps=500, learning_rate=2e-4, bf16=True, optim='adamw_8bit'))
trainer.train()
model.save_pretrained_merged('./out/merged', tok, save_method='merged_16bit')

Inspect outputs: lora adapter, merged 16-bit model, GGUF Q4_K_M for llama.cpp / Ollama.

production_checklist: Always start with a small subset (500-2000 examples) to verify the pipeline before full runs.
Track loss + eval metrics in Weights & Biases - silent overfitting is the most common production failure.
Save adapter checkpoints every 100-500 steps - mid-run crashes lose hours otherwise.
Eval on held-out instructions BEFORE deploying - perplexity is necessary but not sufficient.
Pin unsloth to a specific version - the API changes frequently (it's young).
Use `report_to='wandb'` + `WANDB_PROJECT=ossai-finetune` for team-wide visibility.
For Llama 3.1 specifically, set chat template explicitly - getting it wrong is invisible until inference.

faq.question: LoRA rank - what's the right number?
faq.answer: r=8 is fast but capacity-limited; r=16 is the sweet spot for most tasks; r=32-64 only if you're doing heavy domain transfer (medical, legal). Above 64 the parameter count approaches full fine-tune without the headroom. Always set lora_alpha = r as a starting point.

faq.question: How many examples do I need?
faq.answer: Minimum useful: 500 high-quality instruction-output pairs. Sweet spot: 5k-20k. Above 50k, returns diminish unless you're doing multi-task or domain adaptation. Quality crushes quantity - 1k carefully curated examples beats 100k auto-generated junk.

faq.question: Why Llama 3.1 instead of 3.3?
faq.answer: Llama 3.3 70B is too large for single-GPU LoRA. Llama 3.1 8B is the only Meta model that fits comfortably in 24GB with batch>=1. For 70B fine-tunes, use Axolotl or LLaMA-Factory with multi-GPU.

title: Axolotl QLoRA Fine-Tune (Mixtral 8x7B on 2xA100, DeepSpeed Zero-3)
tldr: Axolotl YAML config for QLoRA fine-tune of Mixtral 8x7B on 2xA100. DeepSpeed Zero-3 sharding, eval callbacks.
description: Production Axolotl recipe to fine-tune Mixtral 8x7B with QLoRA across 2xA100 via DeepSpeed Zero-3 stage 3 offload. Includes eval, checkpointing, and FlashAttention 2.
when_to_use: You have 2-8 A100 80GB cards and need to fine-tune Mixtral 8x7B (or similar 50B+ MoE) with QLoRA. Axolotl + DeepSpeed Zero-3 is the standard multi-GPU stack for 2026 - it abstracts away the FSDP/Zero plumbing and gives you reproducible YAML configs.
when_not_to_use: Skip Axolotl when (1) you only have one GPU - Unsloth is faster on single-card; (2) you're not on NVIDIA - the DeepSpeed stack is CUDA-first; (3) you need maximum control - Axolotl's abstraction can fight you on weird models.
quick_start: 1) `git clone https://github.com/axolotl-ai-cloud/axolotl && cd axolotl && pip install -e '.[deepspeed,flash-attn]'`  2) Save the config YAML  3) `accelerate launch -m axolotl.cli.train mixtral.yml`
full_code:
# mixtral.yml -- Axolotl QLoRA fine-tune of Mixtral 8x7B Instruct on 2xA100 80GB
base_model: mistralai/Mixtral-8x7B-Instruct-v0.1
model_type: AutoModelForCausalLM
tokenizer_type: AutoTokenizer

load_in_4bit: true
adapter: qlora
lora_r: 32
lora_alpha: 16
lora_dropout: 0.05
lora_target_modules:
  - q_proj
  - k_proj
  - v_proj
  - o_proj
  - w1
  - w2
  - w3

# DATA
datasets:
  - path: /data/instructions.jsonl
    type: alpaca         # or chat_template, or completion
    field_instruction: instruction
    field_output: output
dataset_prepared_path: ./prepared_data
val_set_size: 0.02
output_dir: ./out/mixtral-qlora-v1

# TRAINING
sequence_len: 4096
sample_packing: true
pad_to_sequence_len: true

gradient_accumulation_steps: 4
micro_batch_size: 1
num_epochs: 2
optimizer: adamw_torch
lr_scheduler: cosine
learning_rate: 2.0e-4
warmup_ratio: 0.03
weight_decay: 0.01
max_grad_norm: 1.0

bf16: true
flash_attention: true
gradient_checkpointing: true

deepspeed: deepspeed_configs/zero3_bf16.json   # ships with axolotl

# EVAL + LOGGING
evals_per_epoch: 2
logging_steps: 10
save_strategy: steps
save_steps: 500
save_total_limit: 3
wandb_project: ossai-mixtral-qlora
wandb_name: v1-2xa100-zero3

special_tokens:
  pad_token: "</s>"

production_checklist: Pin Axolotl + DeepSpeed + flash-attn versions in a Dockerfile - the stack is brittle across upgrades.
Always test the YAML on a 100-example subset first - YAML typos waste 30 min before failure.
Use sample_packing=true - it cuts training time 30-50% without quality loss.
Track GPU utilization in W&B - if you're <70%, you're data-bottlenecked.
Save checkpoints to S3 / cloud storage in the training loop, not after.
For Mixtral, lora_target_modules must include w1/w2/w3 (MoE FFN), not just attention.
Use deepspeed_configs/zero3_bf16.json (ships with axolotl) - it's already tuned.

faq.question: QLoRA on Mixtral vs LoRA on Llama 70B?
faq.answer: Mixtral QLoRA: ~120GB VRAM total needed across the cluster, 2xA100 80untouchedGB does it with Zero-3. Llama 70B LoRA in 16-bit: 280GB+ needed, requires 4xA100 80GB. QLoRA on 70B is the cheaper path if you can afford the small quality penalty.

faq.question: Axolotl vs LLaMA-Factory vs vanilla HF Trainer?
faq.answer: Axolotl: YAML-driven, opinionated defaults, best for reproducible research-grade runs. LLaMA-Factory: GUI + CLI, friendlier for non-experts, comparable performance. Vanilla HF Trainer: maximum flexibility, most work. For team workflows, Axolotl > LLaMA-Factory > vanilla.

attribution: OSS AI Hub - original work, MIT-licensed.
title: DPO Fine-Tune with TRL (Direct Preference Optimization)
tldr: Direct Preference Optimization on chosen/rejected dataset using TRL DPOTrainer. Beta tuning + eval pipeline.
description: Production DPO recipe: load SFT-tuned model, prepare DPO dataset (prompt + chosen + rejected), train with TRL DPOTrainer, eval on reward + win rate.
when_to_use: You have an SFT-tuned model and preference data (human or AI-judged chosen/rejected pairs). DPO aligns the model to your preferences without a separate reward model - simpler + more stable than PPO/RLHF.
when_not_to_use: Skip DPO when (1) no preference data - just do more SFT; (2) preferences are inconsistent - DPO will learn noise; (3) you need on-policy alignment for safety - PPO/RLHF still wins on tricky safety alignment.
quick_start: 1) `pip install trl transformers datasets bitsandbytes`  2) Prepare a HF dataset with {prompt, chosen, rejected}  3) `python dpo.py`
full_code:
"""DPO fine-tune with TRL DPOTrainer on an SFT-tuned base."""
from __future__ import annotations

import os
from datasets import load_dataset
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM, TrainingArguments
from trl import DPOConfig, DPOTrainer
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model


SFT_MODEL = "chadcorp/llama3_1_8b_ossai_sft_v1"  # your prior SFT output
OUTPUT_DIR = "./outputs/dpo_v1"


def main() -> None:
    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(SFT_MODEL)
    tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.pad_token or tokenizer.eos_token

    model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(SFT_MODEL, torch_dtype="bfloat16", device_map="auto")

    # LoRA on top of SFT model for cheap DPO (full-param DPO needs 2x VRAM for ref model)
    peft_config = LoraConfig(r=16, lora_alpha=16, lora_dropout=0.05, bias="none",
        target_modules=["q_proj", "k_proj", "v_proj", "o_proj"], task_type="CAUSAL_LM")

    # Preference dataset format: {prompt, chosen, rejected}
    ds = load_dataset("Anthropic/hh-rlhf", split="train[:5000]")
    # Anthropic HH has 'chosen'+'rejected' full conversations; convert to {prompt, chosen, rejected} response strings
    def split_pair(ex):
        prompt = ex["chosen"].split("Assistant:")[0] + "Assistant:"
        chosen = ex["chosen"].split("Assistant:", 1)[1].strip()
        rejected = ex["rejected"].split("Assistant:", 1)[1].strip()
        return {"prompt": prompt, "chosen": chosen, "rejected": rejected}
    ds = ds.map(split_pair)

    args = DPOConfig(
        output_dir=OUTPUT_DIR,
        per_device_train_batch_size=2,
        gradient_accumulation_steps=4,
        num_train_epochs=1,
        learning_rate=5e-6,           # DPO needs much lower LR than SFT
        beta=0.1,                     # DPO inverse temperature: 0.1-0.5 typical
        max_length=2048,
        max_prompt_length=1024,
        warmup_ratio=0.03,
        logging_steps=10,
        save_steps=200,
        bf16=True,
        report_to="wandb",
    )
    trainer = DPOTrainer(
        model=model,
        ref_model=None,                # peft will auto-create the ref from base
        args=args,
        tokenizer=tokenizer,
        train_dataset=ds,
        peft_config=peft_config,
    )
    trainer.train()
    trainer.save_model(f"{OUTPUT_DIR}/final")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

production_checklist: ALWAYS start from a strong SFT-tuned base - DPO on a raw base model is unstable.
Eval on held-out preferences + win-rate (AlpacaEval-2, Arena-Hard) BEFORE deploying.
Track chosen_rewards - rejected_rewards in W&B - if it stops growing, halt training.
Use beta=0.1 as default; 0.05 for conservative updates; 0.3+ for aggressive.
Cap epochs at 1-2 - DPO overfits faster than SFT.
Mix in some SFT data during DPO to prevent capability regression (5-10% by token).
Pin trl + peft versions - APIs change between minor versions.

faq.question: DPO vs RLHF (PPO) - which one?
faq.answer: DPO: simpler, more stable, no separate reward model needed. Works great for most alignment tasks. RLHF with PPO: more capable on tricky safety alignment, needs a reward model + PPO infra. For 95% of use cases (style alignment, instruction-following), DPO is the right call. For safety-critical or capability-critical alignment, evaluate both.

faq.question: How much preference data do I need?
faq.answer: Minimum useful: 1k pairs. Sweet spot: 5k-20k. Above 50k, ORPO becomes more compute-efficient. Quality matters most - 1k human-judged pairs >> 100k auto-judged. Use AI-as-judge only if you can validate the judge's calibration.

attribution: OSS AI Hub - original work, MIT-licensed.
title: ORPO Fine-Tune (Single-Stage SFT + Preference Alignment)
tldr: ORPO trains SFT + preference alignment in one stage. 40% less compute than DPO + SFT, comparable quality on Llama 3 70B.
description: Single-stage alignment: ORPO combines instruction tuning + preference optimization without a separate reference model. Best when you have both instruction + preference data.
when_to_use: You're starting from a pretrained base (not SFT) and have both instructions + preference pairs. ORPO finishes both jobs in one training run. Empirically matches DPO-after-SFT quality at ~60% of the total compute.
when_not_to_use: Skip ORPO when (1) you already have a great SFT model and just need preference alignment - DPO is the right next step; (2) your data is instruction-only (no preferences); (3) you need the SFT model separately for ablations.
quick_start: 1) `pip install trl>=0.13`  2) Dataset with prompt+chosen+rejected  3) `python orpo.py`
full_code:
"""ORPO single-stage alignment with TRL."""
from __future__ import annotations

import os
from datasets import load_dataset
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM
from trl import ORPOConfig, ORPOTrainer
from peft import LoraConfig


BASE_MODEL = "meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B"  # Pretrained base, NOT Instruct
OUTPUT_DIR = "./outputs/orpo_v1"


def main() -> None:
    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(BASE_MODEL)
    tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.pad_token or tokenizer.eos_token

    model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(BASE_MODEL, torch_dtype="bfloat16", device_map="auto")

    ds = load_dataset("argilla/ultrafeedback-binarized-preferences", split="train[:10000]")
    # Expected columns: prompt, chosen, rejected

    args = ORPOConfig(
        output_dir=OUTPUT_DIR,
        per_device_train_batch_size=2,
        gradient_accumulation_steps=4,
        num_train_epochs=1,
        learning_rate=8e-6,            # ORPO is between SFT (2e-4) and DPO (5e-6)
        beta=0.1,                      # ORPO odds-ratio weight
        max_length=2048,
        max_prompt_length=1024,
        warmup_ratio=0.03,
        logging_steps=10,
        save_steps=500,
        bf16=True,
        report_to="wandb",
    )
    peft = LoraConfig(r=16, lora_alpha=16, lora_dropout=0.05, bias="none",
        target_modules=["q_proj", "k_proj", "v_proj", "o_proj"], task_type="CAUSAL_LM")

    trainer = ORPOTrainer(
        model=model,
        args=args,
        tokenizer=tokenizer,
        train_dataset=ds,
        peft_config=peft,
    )
    trainer.train()
    trainer.save_model(f"{OUTPUT_DIR}/final")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

faq.question: ORPO vs DPO+SFT?
faq.answer: ORPO: single training run, ~60% compute, comparable quality on most benchmarks. DPO+SFT: two stages, more compute, slightly better fine control + reproducibility. For team workflows wanting fast iterations, ORPO. For research where you compare alignment stages, DPO+SFT.

faq.question: Does ORPO need a reference model?
faq.answer: No - that's the key win. ORPO uses an odds-ratio objective that doesn't need a separate reference model in memory, so VRAM is lower than DPO.

title: LLaMA-Factory Multi-Task Fine-Tune (Web UI + CLI)
tldr: LLaMA-Factory web UI + CLI for multi-task fine-tune across instruction, math, and code datasets. Production-grade with checkpointing.
description: Multi-task fine-tune setup: load LLaMA-Factory, mix datasets via dataset_info.json, train with LoRA + DeepSpeed, eval, export. The easiest path for non-experts.
when_to_use: You want the friendliest multi-task fine-tune stack. LLaMA-Factory has a web UI (Gradio) + YAML configs, supports SFT/DPO/ORPO/KTO, and handles dataset mixing with proportional sampling automatically.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) you need maximum control - LLaMA-Factory hides too much; (2) you're already deep on Axolotl/Unsloth - switching costs time; (3) you need exotic training techniques - the framework lags Axolotl on bleeding edge.
quick_start: 1) `git clone https://github.com/hiyouga/LLaMA-Factory && cd LLaMA-Factory && pip install -e '.[torch,metrics]'`  2) Edit `data/dataset_info.json`  3) `llamafactory-cli train config.yaml`
full_code:
# config.yaml -- LLaMA-Factory multi-task LoRA fine-tune
### model
model_name_or_path: meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct
trust_remote_code: false

### method
stage: sft                  # sft, dpo, orpo, kto, pretraining
do_train: true
finetuning_type: lora
lora_target: all            # adapt all linear layers
lora_rank: 16
lora_alpha: 16
lora_dropout: 0.05

### dataset (multi-task mixing)
dataset: alpaca_en,math_instruct,code_alpaca
# datasets must be registered in data/dataset_info.json
dataset_dir: data
template: llama3
cutoff_len: 4096
max_samples: 30000          # ~10k per dataset weighted
overwrite_cache: true
preprocessing_num_workers: 16

### output
output_dir: saves/llama3-multitask/lora/sft
logging_steps: 10
save_steps: 500
plot_loss: true
overwrite_output_dir: true

### train
per_device_train_batch_size: 2
gradient_accumulation_steps: 4
learning_rate: 2.0e-4
num_train_epochs: 2
lr_scheduler_type: cosine
warmup_ratio: 0.03
bf16: true
flash_attn: fa2
ddp_timeout: 180000000

### eval
val_size: 0.02
per_device_eval_batch_size: 1
eval_strategy: steps
eval_steps: 500

faq.question: LLaMA-Factory vs Axolotl?
faq.answer: LLaMA-Factory: friendlier (web UI), more docs, easier multi-task mixing. Axolotl: closer to research-grade configs, faster iteration on edge cases, slightly more performant. For teams ramping up, LLaMA-Factory is the easier start; for research labs, Axolotl is the standard.

faq.question: Can I use the web UI in production?
faq.answer: Don't - the UI is for exploration. For production, commit a config.yaml + run via CLI in a reproducible Docker image. The UI is great for prototyping the YAML interactively.

title: DeepEval LLM Test Suite (Pytest-Style)
tldr: DeepEval test suite with metrics for faithfulness, answer relevance, bias, and toxicity. CI-integrated, pytest-compatible.
description: Pytest-shaped LLM evaluation with DeepEval: assert metrics per test case, fail CI on regression, get rich HTML reports.
when_to_use: You want LLM quality tests that block CI. DeepEval reads like pytest, runs metrics via Claude/GPT-as-judge, and ships an HTML report. Best fit for chat or RAG apps where you need a regression-prevention safety net.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) you have <50 test cases - manual review beats automated judging at that scale; (2) you don't want LLM-judged metrics (cost + variance) - use exact-match or BLEU instead; (3) you're doing model training - lm-evaluation-harness is the right tool for that.
quick_start: 1) `pip install deepeval`  2) Write a pytest file with @assert_test cases  3) `deepeval test run tests/`
full_code:
"""DeepEval test suite - pytest-style."""
from __future__ import annotations

from deepeval import assert_test
from deepeval.metrics import (
    AnswerRelevancyMetric, FaithfulnessMetric, BiasMetric, ToxicityMetric,
)
from deepeval.test_case import LLMTestCase
import pytest


# Replace with your RAG/agent call
def your_chatbot(question: str, context: list[str]) -> str:
    """Your app code here."""
    return f"Stub answer to: {question}"


@pytest.mark.parametrize("question,context", [
    ("What is your refund policy?", ["Refunds within 30 days. Original packaging required."]),
    ("Do you ship internationally?", ["We ship to US + Canada only."]),
])
def test_refund_qa(question: str, context: list[str]):
    actual = your_chatbot(question, context)
    test_case = LLMTestCase(
        input=question,
        actual_output=actual,
        retrieval_context=context,
    )
    assert_test(test_case, metrics=[
        FaithfulnessMetric(threshold=0.7, model="claude-sonnet-4-5"),
        AnswerRelevancyMetric(threshold=0.7, model="claude-sonnet-4-5"),
        BiasMetric(threshold=0.2),       # higher = more biased; we want low
        ToxicityMetric(threshold=0.1),   # higher = more toxic; we want very low
    ])
code:
"""DeepEval test - core pattern."""
from deepeval import assert_test
from deepeval.metrics import FaithfulnessMetric, AnswerRelevancyMetric
from deepeval.test_case import LLMTestCase


def test_chatbot():
    tc = LLMTestCase(input='Refund policy?', actual_output='...', retrieval_context=['Refunds within 30 days.'])
    assert_test(tc, metrics=[
        FaithfulnessMetric(threshold=0.7, model='claude-sonnet-4-5'),
        AnswerRelevancyMetric(threshold=0.7, model='claude-sonnet-4-5'),
    ])

env_vars.name: OPENAI_API_KEY
env_vars.description: DeepEval defaults to GPT-4o as judge.

setup_steps: Configure judge model (default OpenAI): `deepeval set-azure-openai ...` OR keep default with OPENAI_API_KEY.
Run: `deepeval test run tests/` (or just `pytest tests/`).
Inspect the generated HTML report at `.deepeval-cache/test-runs/...`
Wire into CI: add a `deepeval test run` step that fails the build on threshold breach.

variations.code_snippet:
from deepeval.metrics import GEval
from deepeval.test_case import LLMTestCaseParams
metric = GEval(name='Helpful', criteria='Does it actually help the user complete their task?', evaluation_params=[LLMTestCaseParams.INPUT, LLMTestCaseParams.ACTUAL_OUTPUT], threshold=0.6)

variations.label: Eval an entire RAG pipeline
variations.description: Use ContextualPrecisionMetric + ContextualRecallMetric for retrieval quality.
variations.code_snippet:
from deepeval.metrics import ContextualPrecisionMetric, ContextualRecallMetric
metrics = [ContextualPrecisionMetric(threshold=0.5), ContextualRecallMetric(threshold=0.5)]

common_errors.cause: Running too many cases in parallel against the same API key.
common_errors.fix_snippet:
# In conftest.py:
import deepeval
deepeval.evaluate(test_cases=..., max_concurrent=4)

# Faithfulness requires retrieval_context list:
tc = LLMTestCase(input='...', actual_output='...', retrieval_context=['chunk 1', 'chunk 2'])

common_errors.error_text: Threshold 0.7 keeps failing on subjective tests
common_errors.cause: Judge variance; one bad sample shouldn't fail CI.
common_errors.fix_snippet:
# Average over 3 runs:
metric = AnswerRelevancyMetric(threshold=0.7, model='claude-sonnet-4-5', async_mode=True, strict_mode=False)

production_checklist: Curate at least 50 test cases before relying on DeepEval in CI.
Use Claude Sonnet 4.5 as judge for highest correlation with human ratings.
Cache test results - re-running the same cases is wasteful + nondeterministic.
Track per-metric trends weekly - silent quality regressions are caught here.
Set thresholds conservatively at first (0.5), tighten as you collect baseline data.
Never use the model under test as its own judge - clear bias.
Pin deepeval version - metric implementations change subtly across versions.

faq.question: DeepEval vs RAGAS vs Promptfoo?
faq.answer: DeepEval: pytest-shaped, code-first, best for engineers wiring quality into CI. RAGAS: RAG-specific metrics, simpler API, best for RAG-only eval. Promptfoo: YAML-driven A/B comparisons across models/prompts, best for prompt iteration. Use DeepEval for unit-test-style quality gates, RAGAS for RAG observability, Promptfoo for prompt iteration.

faq.question: Cost of running DeepEval in CI?
faq.answer: Each case typically needs 2-4 judge calls (one per metric). At Claude Sonnet 4.5 rates ($3/1M input), 100 cases * 4 metrics * ~500 input tokens = ~200k tokens = $0.60 per run. Cheap relative to the time it saves catching regressions.

attribution: OSS AI Hub - original work, MIT-licensed.
title: Promptfoo Prompt Regression Testing (A/B Across 5 Models)
tldr: Promptfoo config for A/B testing prompts across 5 models with 100 test cases. Compares cost, latency, and accuracy in one report.
description: YAML-driven prompt eval: declare prompts + providers + test cases + assertions; promptfoo runs all combos in parallel + ships an HTML diff.
when_to_use: You're iterating on prompts and want apples-to-apples comparison across model providers + prompt variants. Promptfoo is the cleanest tool for this in 2026 - one YAML, one command, one report.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) you're testing agents/tools - Promptfoo is prompt-first; (2) you need integration with pytest - DeepEval is cleaner there; (3) you only have one model + one prompt - just eval directly.
quick_start: 1) `npm i -g promptfoo`  2) Save promptfooconfig.yaml  3) `promptfoo eval` then `promptfoo view`
full_code:
# promptfooconfig.yaml -- A/B 2 prompts x 5 models x 100 test cases
description: "OSS support agent prompt regression"

prompts:
  - id: v1
    label: "Production prompt (v1)"
    raw: |
      You are an OSS support agent. Answer the user's question using the context.
      Cite sources. If unsure, say so.
      Context: {{context}}
      Question: {{question}}
  - id: v2
    label: "New prompt (v2)"
    raw: |
      You are a senior OSS support specialist. Answer concisely from the context.
      Always include a verbatim quote when citing. If the context doesn't answer, say "I don't have that information".
      Context: {{context}}
      Question: {{question}}

providers:
  - anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-5
  - anthropic:claude-haiku-4-5
  - openai:gpt-5
  - openai:gpt-4o-mini
  - ollama:llama3.3:70b-instruct-q4_K_M

tests:
  - vars:
      question: "What's the refund window?"
      context: "Refunds within 30 days of purchase."
    assert:
      - type: contains
        value: "30 days"
      - type: not-contains
        value: "I'm sorry, I cannot"
      - type: llm-rubric
        value: "Cites the 30-day window from the context."
        provider: anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-5

  - vars:
      question: "Can I return a digital download?"
      context: "Refunds within 30 days of purchase."
    assert:
      - type: llm-rubric
        value: "Says it doesn't know OR notes the context doesn't address digital downloads."
        provider: anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-5

# Run: promptfoo eval --max-concurrency 5
# View: promptfoo view  (opens an interactive HTML report)
code:
# promptfooconfig.yaml (excerpt)
prompts:
  - id: v1
    raw: 'Answer from context.\nContext: {{context}}\nQuestion: {{question}}'
providers: [anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-5, openai:gpt-4o-mini]
tests:
  - vars: {question: 'Refund policy?', context: 'Refunds within 30 days.'}
    assert:
      - type: contains
        value: '30 days'

production_checklist: Pin model providers + temperature for reproducibility.
Curate at least 50 diverse test cases - 5 examples doesn't reveal regressions.
Run eval before every prompt change - commit results to git for traceability.
Compare cost + latency, not just correctness - cheap model that's 90% as good often wins.
Use --no-cache when changing model versions; otherwise leverage cache.
Pin promptfoo version + commit promptfooconfig.yaml.
Wire `promptfoo eval --output results.json` into CI - fail on regression.

faq.question: Promptfoo vs DeepEval?
faq.answer: Promptfoo: YAML-first, multi-model A/B, best for prompt iteration + provider comparison. DeepEval: pytest-shaped, code-first, best for CI-blocking quality gates on a single deployed app. Most teams use both - Promptfoo when picking prompts/models, DeepEval to keep them honest in production.

faq.question: Can I run against my own local API?
faq.answer: Yes - use `providers: ['http:my-api']` with a custom HTTP config block. Promptfoo supports anything OpenAI-compatible out of the box (vLLM, Ollama, LiteLLM) and arbitrary HTTP for custom endpoints.

title: Ragas RAG Evaluation (4 Metrics, Fully Automated)
tldr: Ragas pipeline: faithfulness + answer relevance + context precision + context recall - all LLM-judged. Outputs CSV for dashboards.
description: Production Ragas eval harness: load eval set (question + ground_truth + retrieved_contexts + answer), score with 4 core metrics, export per-row + aggregate CSV.
when_to_use: You shipped a RAG and need to track quality over time. Ragas's 4 metrics cover the core failure modes: hallucination (faithfulness), off-topic answer (relevance), bad retrieval (precision/recall). Run weekly on a held-out set + dashboard the deltas.
when_not_to_use: Skip Ragas when (1) you don't have ground_truth labels - precision/recall metrics need them; (2) your judge model is weaker than the generator - judge variance dominates the signal; (3) your stack isn't RAG.
quick_start: 1) `pip install ragas datasets`  2) Prepare eval set with question/ground_truth/contexts/answer  3) `python ragas_eval.py`
full_code:
"""Ragas RAG evaluation - 4 core metrics."""
from __future__ import annotations

import os
from datasets import Dataset
import pandas as pd
from ragas import evaluate
from ragas.metrics import faithfulness, answer_relevancy, context_precision, context_recall
from ragas.llms import LangchainLLMWrapper
from ragas.embeddings import LangchainEmbeddingsWrapper
from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings


# Eval set columns: question, ground_truth (gold answer), contexts (retrieved chunks), answer (system output)
SAMPLE = {
    "question": [
        "What's the refund window?",
        "Do you ship internationally?",
    ],
    "ground_truth": [
        "30 days from purchase.",
        "US and Canada only.",
    ],
    "contexts": [
        ["Refunds within 30 days of purchase. Original packaging required."],
        ["We ship to US + Canada only. International shipping is not available."],
    ],
    "answer": [
        "You can request a refund within 30 days of purchase.",
        "We currently only ship to the US and Canada.",
    ],
}


def main() -> None:
    ds = Dataset.from_dict(SAMPLE)
    judge = LangchainLLMWrapper(ChatAnthropic(model="claude-sonnet-4-5", temperature=0))
    embed = LangchainEmbeddingsWrapper(OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small"))

    metrics = [faithfulness, answer_relevancy, context_precision, context_recall]
    result = evaluate(ds, metrics=metrics, llm=judge, embeddings=embed)

    df = result.to_pandas()
    print(df)
    df.to_csv("ragas_eval_results.csv", index=False)

    # Aggregate
    print("\n=== Aggregate ===")
    for m in metrics:
        print(f"{m.name}: {df[m.name].mean():.3f}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

production_checklist: Curate at least 100 ground-truth-labeled examples - smaller sets have noisy aggregate metrics.
Use temperature=0 on the judge - non-zero turns Ragas results into a slot machine.
Run Ragas weekly on a frozen golden set + ad-hoc on prod traffic samples.
Track per-question scores - aggregate hides specific regressions.
Pin Ragas minor version - 0.1.x to 0.2.x changed metric internals.
Avoid using the same model for generation and judging - clear bias.
Cache judge calls aggressively - same (question, answer, contexts) triple repeats often.

faq.question: Which Ragas metric matters most?
faq.answer: For preventing hallucination: faithfulness. For preventing off-topic answers: answer_relevancy. For diagnosing retrieval: context_precision (junk retrieved?) + context_recall (gold context missing?). Faithfulness is the one most teams gate releases on.

faq.question: Do I need ground_truth labels?
faq.answer: For faithfulness + answer_relevancy + context_precision: no. For context_recall + answer_correctness: yes. If labeling is expensive, drop to the 3 unsupervised metrics and add human spot-checks weekly.

title: lm-evaluation-harness on Custom Tasks (MMLU + HumanEval + Yours)
tldr: Run MMLU + HumanEval + custom YAML tasks on any HF model. JSON results parser + comparison plot script.
description: EleutherAI's lm-evaluation-harness: the standard academic benchmark runner. Add a YAML task for your domain, run all benchmarks, compare models with one command.
when_to_use: You're comparing fine-tuned models to each other and to baselines (Llama 3.1 base, Llama 3.3 instruct, Mixtral). lm-evaluation-harness is the gold-standard runner - if you cite MMLU/HumanEval/GSM8K numbers, this is how the rest of the field measured them.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) you're eval'ing applied quality (chat, agents) - DeepEval/Ragas are right; (2) you need streaming/long-context evals - harness is single-turn; (3) you want pretty dashboards - the JSON output needs a viz layer.
quick_start: 1) `pip install lm-eval`  2) `lm_eval --model hf --model_args pretrained=meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct --tasks mmlu,hellaswag,humaneval --batch_size auto`
full_code:
"""lm-evaluation-harness with a custom YAML task + JSON comparison."""
import json
import subprocess
from pathlib import Path


def run_harness(model_id: str, tasks: list[str], output: str, batch_size: str = "auto") -> dict:
    cmd = [
        "lm_eval",
        "--model", "hf",
        "--model_args", f"pretrained={model_id},dtype=bfloat16",
        "--tasks", ",".join(tasks),
        "--batch_size", batch_size,
        "--output_path", output,
        "--log_samples",
    ]
    print("[lm_eval]", " ".join(cmd))
    subprocess.run(cmd, check=True)
    result_file = list(Path(output).glob(f"**/results_*.json"))[-1]
    return json.loads(result_file.read_text())


def compare(model_a: str, model_b: str, tasks: list[str]) -> None:
    a = run_harness(model_a, tasks, "./out/a")
    b = run_harness(model_b, tasks, "./out/b")
    print(f"\n{'task':<25} {model_a:<35} {model_b:<35}")
    for task in tasks:
        sa = a["results"][task].get("acc,none", a["results"][task].get("acc"))
        sb = b["results"][task].get("acc,none", b["results"][task].get("acc"))
        delta = (sb - sa) * 100 if sa and sb else 0
        print(f"{task:<25} {sa:<35.4f} {sb:<35.4f}  delta {delta:+.2f}pp")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    TASKS = ["mmlu", "hellaswag", "humaneval"]
    compare(
        model_a="meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct",
        model_b="chadcorp/llama3_1_8b_ossai_v1",
        tasks=TASKS,
    )

production_checklist: Pin lm-eval version + commit the exact command - benchmark numbers are version-sensitive.
Use --log_samples for reproducibility - lets you investigate per-question failures.
Run on a known checkpoint (e.g. meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct) periodically to catch silent regressions in harness.
Use vLLM backend for >7B models - HF backend is 10-50x slower.
Cache results by (model_id, task, version) hash - reruns are wasteful.
For published numbers, fix --num_fewshot per task (MMLU uses 5-shot canonically).
Watch metric naming - 'acc' vs 'acc,none' vs 'em' varies by task.

faq.question: Why are my MMLU numbers lower than the paper?
faq.answer: Check (a) --num_fewshot 5 (the canonical setting), (b) the harness version (numbers shift slightly across versions), (c) model dtype (bf16 vs fp16 can change a few tenths), and (d) whether you used the chat template (instruct models need it). Reproducing published numbers is finicky - use the exact command from the model card.

faq.question: Is lm-eval-harness right for production eval?
faq.answer: Not directly - academic benchmarks measure general capability, not your specific use case. Use harness to compare base models pre-fine-tune, then use DeepEval/Ragas for applied quality on your domain.

title: Golden Dataset A/B Harness for Prompt Changes
tldr: Pytest harness that runs each prompt variant against a golden dataset, computes deltas, fails CI on regression beyond threshold.
description: Lightweight pure-pytest A/B harness: load gold cases, call variant A + variant B in parallel, compute metric deltas, fail the build on regression.
when_to_use: Simplest possible CI quality gate: you have 50-500 gold (input, expected_keywords) pairs and want a pure-pytest harness that prevents prompt regressions. No external eval framework needed.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) you need LLM-judged metrics - DeepEval/Promptfoo are right; (2) you need multi-model comparison - Promptfoo wins; (3) your eval is purely subjective - keyword/regex matching won't help.
quick_start: 1) `pip install pytest`  2) Save `golden_cases.jsonl` + `test_prompts.py`  3) `pytest -v --regression-threshold=0.05`
full_code:
"""Pure-pytest A/B harness against a golden dataset."""
from __future__ import annotations

import json
import pathlib
import re
from typing import Iterable

import pytest


GOLDEN_PATH = pathlib.Path("golden_cases.jsonl")


def load_cases() -> list[dict]:
    return [json.loads(ln) for ln in GOLDEN_PATH.read_text(encoding="utf-8").splitlines() if ln.strip()]


def chat(prompt_template: str, question: str) -> str:
    """Plug in your model call here."""
    # Stub - replace with your actual LLM call.
    return f"Stub answer using {prompt_template[:15]}... for: {question}"


def score(answer: str, expected_keywords: list[str], banned_keywords: list[str]) -> float:
    """Simple keyword + regex score. Replace with your domain scorer."""
    hits = sum(1 for k in expected_keywords if k.lower() in answer.lower())
    banned = sum(1 for k in banned_keywords if k.lower() in answer.lower())
    return max(0.0, (hits / max(len(expected_keywords), 1)) - (banned * 0.5))


PROMPT_V1 = "You are a support agent. Answer the user's question.\nQ: {q}\nA:"
PROMPT_V2 = "You are a senior support specialist. Answer concisely from the context.\nQ: {q}\nA:"


@pytest.fixture(scope="session")
def cases():
    return load_cases()


def _score_variant(cases: list[dict], template: str) -> float:
    scores = [score(chat(template, c["question"]), c["expected_keywords"], c.get("banned_keywords", [])) for c in cases]
    return sum(scores) / len(scores)


def test_v2_does_not_regress(cases):
    v1_score = _score_variant(cases, PROMPT_V1)
    v2_score = _score_variant(cases, PROMPT_V2)
    regression = v1_score - v2_score
    threshold = 0.05  # max acceptable 5pp drop
    print(f"\nV1={v1_score:.3f}  V2={v2_score:.3f}  regression={regression*100:+.1f}pp")
    assert regression <= threshold, f"V2 regressed {regression*100:.1f}pp > {threshold*100:.0f}pp threshold"

import json, pathlib, pytest

GOLDEN = pathlib.Path('golden_cases.jsonl')

def load_cases():
    return [json.loads(l) for l in GOLDEN.read_text().splitlines() if l.strip()]

def test_no_regression():
    cases = load_cases()
    v1_avg = sum(score(call(P_V1, c)) for c in cases) / len(cases)
    v2_avg = sum(score(call(P_V2, c)) for c in cases) / len(cases)
    assert v1_avg - v2_avg <= 0.05, 'V2 regressed > 5pp'

production_checklist: Curate 50-200 golden cases - any fewer is noisy; any more is hard to maintain.
Set temperature=0 on the LLM call so the test is deterministic.
Pick a regression threshold based on natural noise (run V1 twice, measure variance).
Run on every prompt change - this catches the silent regressions DeepEval misses.
Version golden_cases.jsonl in git - your eval set is part of the product.
When the test fails, manually review the diffs - sometimes V2 is actually better and your golden set needs updating.
Combine with DeepEval for richer metrics on the same set.

faq.question: Why pure pytest instead of DeepEval/Promptfoo?
faq.answer: Less moving parts. For solo devs and small teams, the simplest harness wins because you'll actually run it. Add LLM-judged metrics later when the simple harness stops catching enough regressions.

faq.question: How big should the golden set be?
faq.answer: Minimum 50 cases for stable averages. Sweet spot 100-200. Above 500 the harness becomes slow + you lose the agility - move to DeepEval at that scale.

title: Langfuse Self-Hosted LLM Tracing (Docker Compose + Python SDK)
tldr: Self-hosted Langfuse: docker-compose with Postgres + ClickHouse, Python SDK trace decorator, React dashboard. The OSS LLM observability standard for 2026.
description: Production Langfuse stack: web + worker containers + Postgres + ClickHouse (for cheap span storage), plus the @observe decorator and a sample RAG trace.
when_to_use: You need first-class LLM tracing (prompt, response, tokens, cost, latency per span) self-hosted on your infra. Langfuse v3 ships ClickHouse for cheap long-retention, has the cleanest UI of any open-source LLM observability tool, and integrates with every major SDK.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) you have <100 LLM calls/day - the infra overhead isn't worth it; (2) you're all-in on a vendor stack (Datadog LLM Observability, Helicone Cloud); (3) you only need cost tracking - simpler proxies (Helicone) suffice.
quick_start: 1) `git clone https://github.com/langfuse/langfuse && docker compose up -d`  2) `pip install langfuse`  3) Set LANGFUSE_PUBLIC_KEY + SECRET_KEY  4) Decorate your function with @observe.
full_code:
"""Langfuse self-hosted: docker-compose stub + Python SDK wiring."""
# ---- docker-compose.yml (run via `docker compose up -d`) ----
# Save as docker-compose.yml in the repo root.
#
# version: "3.8"
# services:
#   langfuse-server:
#     image: langfuse/langfuse:3
#     depends_on: [postgres, clickhouse]
#     ports: ["3000:3000"]
#     environment:
#       DATABASE_URL: postgresql://postgres:postgres@postgres:5432/postgres
#       CLICKHOUSE_URL: http://clickhouse:8123
#       CLICKHOUSE_MIGRATION_URL: clickhouse://clickhouse:9000
#       NEXTAUTH_SECRET: "${NEXTAUTH_SECRET}"
#       NEXTAUTH_URL: http://localhost:3000
#       SALT: "${SALT}"
#   postgres:
#     image: postgres:16
#     environment: { POSTGRES_PASSWORD: postgres }
#     volumes: [pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data]
#   clickhouse:
#     image: clickhouse/clickhouse-server:24
#     volumes: [chdata:/var/lib/clickhouse]
# volumes: { pgdata: {}, chdata: {} }


# ---- Python SDK: trace every LLM call automatically ----
from __future__ import annotations

import os
from langfuse import observe, Langfuse
from anthropic import Anthropic


langfuse = Langfuse(
    public_key=os.environ["LANGFUSE_PUBLIC_KEY"],
    secret_key=os.environ["LANGFUSE_SECRET_KEY"],
    host=os.environ.get("LANGFUSE_HOST", "http://localhost:3000"),
)
claude = Anthropic()


@observe(name="rag_answer", as_type="generation")
def answer(question: str, contexts: list[str]) -> str:
    """Annotated with @observe - Langfuse captures input, output, tokens, latency automatically."""
    ctx = "\n\n".join(f"[{i+1}] {c}" for i, c in enumerate(contexts))
    msg = claude.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-5",
        max_tokens=500,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Answer from context only.\n\nContext:\n{ctx}\n\nQuestion: {question}"}],
    )
    # Optional: attach scores / metadata
    langfuse.score(name="manual_quality_score", value=None)  # leave for human review
    return msg.content[0].text


if __name__ == "__main__":
    out = answer("What is the refund window?", ["Refunds are issued within 30 days of purchase."])
    print(out)
    langfuse.flush()  # ensure spans are sent before process exit
code:
from langfuse import observe, Langfuse
from anthropic import Anthropic
import os

langfuse = Langfuse(public_key=os.environ['LANGFUSE_PUBLIC_KEY'], secret_key=os.environ['LANGFUSE_SECRET_KEY'], host='http://localhost:3000')
claude = Anthropic()

@observe(name='rag_answer', as_type='generation')
def answer(question, contexts):
    ctx = '\n'.join(contexts)
    msg = claude.messages.create(model='claude-sonnet-4-5', max_tokens=500,
        messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': f'Answer from context.\nContext: {ctx}\nQ: {question}'}])
    return msg.content[0].text

print(answer('Refund window?', ['Refunds within 30 days.']))
langfuse.flush()

production_checklist: Use Langfuse v3 with ClickHouse - the Postgres-only v2 doesn't scale past 1M traces.
Rotate the secret + public keys per environment (dev/staging/prod).
Tag traces with environment + user_id - filtering matters at scale.
Set retention policy in Langfuse UI - default keeps everything forever.
Monitor the worker container - it batches spans; if it dies, spans pile up.
Back up Postgres + ClickHouse volumes separately - they hold different data.
Wire prompt versions through Langfuse's prompt registry - lets you A/B without code changes.

faq.question: Langfuse vs Phoenix vs Helicone?
faq.answer: Langfuse: best UI + prompt registry + datasets + evals - the all-in-one. Phoenix (Arize): best OTel native - integrates with existing observability stacks. Helicone: simplest setup (one-line proxy) - best for cost tracking + basic logging only. For new self-hosted setups in 2026, Langfuse is the default.

faq.question: Do I need ClickHouse?
faq.answer: For Langfuse v3: yes. v2 was Postgres-only and limited to ~1M traces. v3's hybrid storage (Postgres for projects/users/prompts + ClickHouse for spans) is the only way to keep <100ms query latency past a few weeks of production traffic.

title: Phoenix (Arize) LLM Tracing with OpenTelemetry
tldr: Arize Phoenix self-hosted with OTel collector + LLM semantic conventions. Auto-instruments OpenAI/Anthropic/LangChain.
description: Phoenix is the OTel-native LLM observability tool. Self-host the collector + UI, drop in OpenInference instrumentors, and every LLM call appears as standardized spans.
when_to_use: You already run an OTel stack (Datadog, Grafana Tempo, Honeycomb) and want LLM spans to flow into it via standard semantic conventions. Phoenix is the only LLM observability tool that's truly OTel-native - other tools bolt OTel on as an export option.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) you need a prompt registry / datasets UI - Langfuse is friendlier; (2) you don't have an existing observability stack - Phoenix is bare-bones outside spans; (3) you want all-in-one setup.
quick_start: 1) `pip install arize-phoenix openinference-instrumentation-anthropic`  2) Run `phoenix serve` in another terminal  3) Instrument your code with one import.
full_code:
"""Phoenix (Arize) OTel tracing for Anthropic + OpenAI + LangChain."""
from __future__ import annotations

import os
from openinference.instrumentation.anthropic import AnthropicInstrumentor
from openinference.instrumentation.openai import OpenAIInstrumentor
import phoenix as px
from phoenix.otel import register


# Option 1: launch local Phoenix UI (development)
session = px.launch_app()  # opens http://localhost:6006

# Option 2: send to a remote Phoenix collector (production)
# os.environ["PHOENIX_COLLECTOR_ENDPOINT"] = "http://phoenix.internal:6006"


# Register the OTel tracer provider
tracer_provider = register(
    project_name="ossai-rag",
    endpoint=os.environ.get("PHOENIX_COLLECTOR_ENDPOINT", "http://localhost:6006/v1/traces"),
)

# One-line auto-instrumentation - every call becomes an OTel span
AnthropicInstrumentor().instrument(tracer_provider=tracer_provider)
OpenAIInstrumentor().instrument(tracer_provider=tracer_provider)


# Your code runs unchanged - spans appear in Phoenix automatically
from anthropic import Anthropic

claude = Anthropic()
result = claude.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-5",
    max_tokens=500,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What is OpenTelemetry?"}],
)
print(result.content[0].text)


# Custom spans for non-LLM steps in your pipeline
from opentelemetry import trace
tracer = trace.get_tracer(__name__)

with tracer.start_as_current_span("retrieval") as span:
    span.set_attribute("retrieval.k", 5)
    span.set_attribute("retrieval.score_threshold", 0.55)
    # ... your retrieval code
    span.set_attribute("retrieval.num_results", 5)


# Inspect spans in browser: localhost:6006
# Export to Datadog / Honeycomb / Grafana Tempo by pointing PHOENIX_COLLECTOR_ENDPOINT
# at your OTel collector (same semantic conventions flow through).

production_checklist: Always run Phoenix collector + UI as separate containers in prod - the launch_app() dev mode is in-process only.
Use BatchSpanProcessor (default in `register()`) - SimpleSpanProcessor kills throughput.
Tag spans with environment + tenant_id + user_id for filtering.
Set sample rate < 1.0 at scale: `tracer_provider.add_span_processor(BatchSpanProcessor(exporter, sampler=TraceIdRatioBased(0.1)))`
Forward to your central observability stack via OTLP - Phoenix is a local lens, not your system of record.
Pin openinference-instrumentation-* versions - they break with provider SDK changes.
Use Phoenix's eval datasets feature to attach quality scores to historical spans.

faq.question: Phoenix vs Langfuse - which observability tool?
faq.answer: Phoenix: OTel-native, best when you already have an OTel pipeline (Datadog/Tempo/Honeycomb). Langfuse: richer UI + prompt registry + datasets, best as a standalone LLM platform. If you'd otherwise build OTel forwarding yourself, Phoenix is one less system. If you need prompt management + evals + traces in one UI, Langfuse wins.

faq.question: Can I use Phoenix without OTel infrastructure?
faq.answer: Yes - it runs standalone as a local UI. But the value compounds when you forward spans to your OTel stack: same semantic conventions, ingest into Datadog/Honeycomb/Tempo, query alongside your service spans.
