attribution: OSS AI Hub - original work, MIT-licensed.
title: KTO Fine-Tune (Binary Thumbs-Up/Down Feedback)
tldr: KTO alignment using binary labels (good/bad) instead of paired preferences. Easier data collection than DPO.
description: Kahneman-Tversky Optimization: align with just {prompt, completion, label} instead of {prompt, chosen, rejected}. Works when your feedback is thumbs-up/down not pairs.
when_to_use: You collect thumbs-up/thumbs-down feedback from users (chat apps, recommender systems). KTO uses this signal directly - no need to construct chosen/rejected pairs. Drastically simplifies the data collection pipeline.
when_not_to_use: Skip KTO when (1) you have paired preferences - DPO is simpler; (2) feedback is very imbalanced (95% positive) - KTO struggles; (3) you can collect Likert (1-5) - use IPO or direct reward modeling instead.
quick_start: 1) `pip install trl>=0.13`  2) Dataset with {prompt, completion, label: True/False}  3) `python kto.py`
full_code:
"""KTO fine-tune on binary feedback."""
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM
from trl import KTOConfig, KTOTrainer
from peft import LoraConfig
from datasets import load_dataset


MODEL = "chadcorp/llama3_1_8b_ossai_sft_v1"

tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(MODEL)
tok.pad_token = tok.pad_token or tok.eos_token
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(MODEL, torch_dtype="bfloat16", device_map="auto")

# Dataset columns: prompt, completion, label (bool: True=thumbs-up, False=thumbs-down)
ds = load_dataset("argilla/ultrafeedback-kto-cleaned", split="train[:10000]")

trainer = KTOTrainer(
    model=model,
    args=KTOConfig(
        output_dir="./out/kto",
        per_device_train_batch_size=4,
        gradient_accumulation_steps=2,
        num_train_epochs=1,
        learning_rate=5e-6,
        beta=0.1,
        max_length=2048,
        max_prompt_length=1024,
        warmup_ratio=0.03,
        bf16=True,
        # KTO-specific: importance weights to balance positive vs negative samples
        desirable_weight=1.0,
        undesirable_weight=1.0,
    ),
    tokenizer=tok,
    train_dataset=ds,
    peft_config=LoraConfig(r=16, lora_alpha=16, target_modules=["q_proj","k_proj","v_proj","o_proj"]),
)
trainer.train()
trainer.save_model("./out/kto/final")

Eval the result against the SFT baseline on your domain.
Tune desirable_weight / undesirable_weight if your data is imbalanced.

common_errors.cause: All samples labeled True OR undesirable_weight too low.

KTOConfig(undesirable_weight=3.0)

common_errors.error_text: Loss is NaN early in training
common_errors.cause: Learning rate too high for the model + LoRA combo.
common_errors.fix_snippet: learning_rate=1e-6  # was 5e-6

common_errors.error_text: Eval shows worse than baseline SFT

# Reduce beta (less aggressive update):
beta=0.05  # was 0.1

production_checklist: Balance your data - 30-70% positives is healthy; otherwise use weights.
Start from SFT model, NOT base - KTO assumes instruction-following exists.
beta=0.1 default; 0.05 conservative, 0.3 aggressive.
Always compare against SFT-baseline on eval set before shipping.
Pin trl version - KTO API has been moving.
Use small batches + grad accum - KTO can be unstable at large effective batch sizes.
Sample from a held-out feedback set for the eval - don't reuse training data.

faq.question: KTO vs DPO when I have both kinds of data?
faq.answer: If you have paired preferences AND binary feedback: combine them. Convert paired into binary (chosen=True, rejected=False) and feed both to KTOTrainer. You get more data for the same alignment effect.

faq.question: Is KTO better than DPO?
faq.answer: On paired data, DPO and KTO are roughly equivalent. KTO's edge is enabling alignment on data you couldn't otherwise use (binary feedback from production). Use whichever matches your data shape.

attribution: OSS AI Hub - original work, MIT-licensed.
title: GaLore Full Fine-Tune on a 24GB GPU (7B Model)
tldr: GaLore gradient-projection trick: full-parameter fine-tune of 7B models on consumer 24GB GPUs. Better quality than LoRA.
description: GaLore projects gradients onto a low-rank subspace, slashing optimizer memory. Lets you do full-parameter fine-tuning on a 24GB RTX 4090 - LoRA-level VRAM, full-parameter quality.
when_to_use: You want full-parameter fine-tune quality on a consumer GPU (no LoRA bottleneck) without paying for cloud H100s. GaLore on a 4090 fits a full 7B fine-tune in ~22GB VRAM - same memory as a LoRA-r64 run but ~3-5% better on most evals.
when_not_to_use: Skip GaLore when (1) LoRA results are already great - simpler; (2) you need to fine-tune 70B - GaLore alone doesn't scale that high; (3) you need 2x training speed - Unsloth+LoRA is faster.
quick_start: 1) `pip install transformers galore-torch`  2) Replace optimizer with GaLoreAdamW  3) Run with full-param fine-tune.
full_code:
"""GaLore full-param fine-tune of Llama 3 7B on a 24GB GPU."""
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM, Trainer, TrainingArguments
from datasets import load_dataset
from galore_torch import GaLoreAdamW

MODEL = "meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct"
tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(MODEL)
tok.pad_token = tok.eos_token
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(MODEL, torch_dtype="bfloat16", attn_implementation="flash_attention_2")

ds = load_dataset("yahma/alpaca-cleaned", split="train[:5000]")
def fmt(ex):
    return {"text": tok.apply_chat_template([{"role":"user","content":ex["instruction"]},{"role":"assistant","content":ex["output"]}], tokenize=False)}
ds = ds.map(fmt)
ds = ds.map(lambda e: tok(e["text"], truncation=True, max_length=2048), batched=True)


# Split params: GaLore-projected (large matrices) vs regular (everything else)
galore_params = []; regular_params = []
target_modules = ["q_proj", "k_proj", "v_proj", "o_proj", "gate_proj", "up_proj", "down_proj"]
for n, p in model.named_parameters():
    if any(t in n for t in target_modules):
        galore_params.append(p)
    else:
        regular_params.append(p)


param_groups = [
    {"params": regular_params},
    {"params": galore_params, "rank": 256, "update_proj_gap": 200, "scale": 0.25, "proj_type": "std"},
]
optimizer = GaLoreAdamW(param_groups, lr=2e-5, weight_decay=0.01)

trainer = Trainer(
    model=model,
    tokenizer=tok,
    train_dataset=ds,
    optimizers=(optimizer, None),
    args=TrainingArguments(
        output_dir="./out/galore",
        per_device_train_batch_size=1,
        gradient_accumulation_steps=16,
        num_train_epochs=2,
        bf16=True,
        gradient_checkpointing=True,
        logging_steps=10,
        save_steps=500,
    ),
)
trainer.train()
trainer.save_model("./out/galore/final")

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM, Trainer, TrainingArguments
from galore_torch import GaLoreAdamW

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained('meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct', torch_dtype='bfloat16')
galore_params = [p for n,p in model.named_parameters() if any(t in n for t in ['q_proj','k_proj','v_proj','o_proj'])]
regular_params = [p for n,p in model.named_parameters() if p not in galore_params]

opt = GaLoreAdamW([
    {'params': regular_params},
    {'params': galore_params, 'rank': 256, 'update_proj_gap': 200, 'scale': 0.25, 'proj_type': 'std'},
], lr=2e-5)

production_checklist: Use rank=256 as the starting point - drop to 128 for tight VRAM, raise to 512 for quality.
Use 10x lower LR than LoRA - GaLore is sensitive.
Pair with FlashAttention 2 + gradient checkpointing for max VRAM savings.
Eval against LoRA + full-param baselines - GaLore wins on some tasks, ties on others.
GaLore's main appeal is VRAM, not speed - expect 1.5-3x slower per step vs LoRA.
Save checkpoints every 200 steps - the long runs lose hours on crashes.
Pin galore-torch version - the project is young.

faq.question: GaLore vs LoRA vs QLoRA - which?
faq.answer: LoRA: simplest, fastest, slight quality penalty vs full-param. QLoRA: LoRA + 4-bit base, even less VRAM, marginally lower quality. GaLore: full-param quality at LoRA-ish VRAM, ~2x slower than LoRA. Use GaLore when you measurably need full-param quality and don't have H100 budget.

faq.question: Why isn't everyone using GaLore?
faq.answer: Three reasons: (1) it's 2-3x slower than LoRA, (2) the optimizer + projection introduce hyperparameter complexity (rank, gap, scale, proj_type), (3) LoRA results are 'good enough' for most tasks. GaLore shines on hard tasks where 3-5% matters.

title: Arena-Hard ELO Benchmark Runner (Compare to LMSys Arena)
tldr: Run Arena-Hard-Auto eval against your model, get an ELO rating comparable to LMSys Arena. Useful for model selection.
description: Arena-Hard-Auto is a 500-prompt benchmark designed to correlate with LMSys Chatbot Arena ELO. Run it on your fine-tune to compare to GPT-5/Claude/Llama scores.
when_to_use: You fine-tuned a chat model and want to know where it lands relative to public models on chat quality. Arena-Hard's 500 hard prompts + auto-judge produces an ELO that correlates ~0.98 with LMSys Arena - much cheaper than human evals.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) you have a narrow domain - Arena-Hard prompts are general; (2) you need per-task metrics - Arena-Hard outputs one number; (3) cost-sensitive - judging costs $5-20 per run.
quick_start: 1) `git clone https://github.com/lmarena/arena-hard-auto`  2) Generate completions  3) Judge with stronger model  4) Compute ELO.
full_code:
"""Arena-Hard-Auto runner against your endpoint."""
import json, subprocess
from pathlib import Path


# 1. Generate model answers
def gen_model_answers(model_id: str, endpoint: str, out: Path):
    """For each Arena-Hard prompt, query your model and save answer."""
    prompts = json.loads(Path("data/arena-hard-v0.1/question.jsonl").read_text())
    answers = []
    for p in prompts:
        # call your model
        import httpx
        r = httpx.post(endpoint, json={"model": model_id, "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": p["turns"][0]["content"]}], "max_tokens": 4096}, timeout=120)
        answers.append({"question_id": p["question_id"], "answer_id": f"{model_id}_{p['question_id']}", "model_id": model_id, "choices": [{"index": 0, "turns": [{"content": r.json()["choices"][0]["message"]["content"]}]}]})
    Path(out).write_text("\n".join(json.dumps(a) for a in answers))


# 2. Judge with stronger model (the Arena-Hard repo provides gen_judgment.py)
# python gen_judgment.py --model your_model --baseline gpt-4-0314 --judge gpt-4-1106-preview
def run_judge(model_id: str, baseline: str = "gpt-4-0314", judge: str = "gpt-5"):
    subprocess.run(["python", "gen_judgment.py", "--model", model_id, "--baseline", baseline, "--judge", judge], check=True)


# 3. Compute ELO
# python show_result.py
def show_results():
    subprocess.run(["python", "show_result.py"], check=True)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    gen_model_answers("ossai-llama-v1", "http://localhost:8000/v1/chat/completions", Path("data/arena-hard-v0.1/model_answer/ossai-llama-v1.jsonl"))
    run_judge("ossai-llama-v1")
    show_results()

production_checklist: Always use temperature=0 on both model and judge for reproducibility.
Pin the judge model version - 'gpt-5' moves; 'gpt-5-2026-05-01' doesn't.
Bootstrap ELO for confidence intervals - single run can vary +/- 5 ELO.

Run after every meaningful training change - track ELO over time in W&B.
Don't over-fit to Arena-Hard - it's a benchmark, not your eval set.

faq.question: Arena-Hard vs MMLU?
faq.answer: MMLU: knowledge benchmark, 4-choice multi-choice, deterministic eval. Arena-Hard: open-ended chat quality, LLM-judged, correlates with user preference. Both have value - MMLU for capability, Arena-Hard for preference.

faq.question: How does Arena-Hard ELO map to LMSys Arena ELO?
faq.answer: They correlate ~0.98 (per the Arena-Hard paper). Numerically close but not identical - Arena-Hard tends to be ~50-100 ELO lower than LMSys for the same model. Use it as a leading indicator.

title: HumanEval / HumanEval+ / MBPP Code Benchmark Runner
tldr: Run HumanEval / HumanEval+ / MBPP on any code LLM. Docker sandbox for safe code execution + pass@k computation.
description: bigcode-evaluation-harness pattern: load benchmark, generate completions, execute in sandbox, compute pass@1, pass@10, pass@100.
when_to_use: You're evaluating a code model (Llama 3.1, Qwen2.5-Coder, DeepSeek-Coder, your fine-tune). HumanEval is the standard - 164 hand-written Python problems with hidden tests; HumanEval+ has 80x more tests per problem.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) you need long-context code eval (CodeAct/SWE-bench better); (2) non-Python code (use MultiPL-E); (3) novel benchmark needed - HumanEval is leaked into training corpora.
quick_start: 1) `pip install bigcode-evaluation-harness`  2) Docker running for sandbox  3) `bigcode-eval --tasks humanevalplus --model your-model`
full_code:
"""HumanEval+ runner with Docker sandbox via bigcode-evaluation-harness."""
import subprocess

# 1. Generate completions (uses vLLM as backend for speed)
subprocess.run([
    "python", "-m", "bigcode_eval.main",
    "--model", "meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct",
    "--tasks", "humanevalplus,mbpp",
    "--max_length_generation", "1024",
    "--temperature", "0.2",
    "--n_samples", "10",            # for pass@10
    "--allow_code_execution",        # required for actual code eval
    "--use_auth_token",
    "--save_generations",
    "--save_generations_path", "./generations.jsonl",
    "--metric_output_path", "./metrics.json",
], check=True)

# Read results
import json
metrics = json.loads(open("./metrics.json").read())
for task, results in metrics.items():
    if "pass@1" in results:
        print(f"{task}: pass@1={results['pass@1']:.3f}  pass@10={results.get('pass@10', 'N/A')}")

production_checklist: Always use HumanEval+ over original HumanEval - +80x more tests, less false-positive pass@1.
Run pass@1 + pass@10 - large gap means model is unstable.
Use temperature=0 for pass@1 reporting, 0.2-0.8 for pass@k.

faq.question: HumanEval vs MBPP vs LiveCodeBench?
faq.answer: HumanEval: 164 problems, Python only, the standard. MBPP: 974 problems, lower difficulty, more contamination. LiveCodeBench: monthly new problems from LeetCode, lowest contamination but smallest. Run all three for a complete picture.

faq.question: Why are HumanEval+ scores lower than HumanEval?
faq.answer: HumanEval+ adds 80x more test cases per problem. Many models pass the canonical tests but fail edge cases. HumanEval+ is what you should trust.

title: SWE-bench Verified Agent Runner
tldr: Run a coding agent against SWE-bench Verified (500 real GitHub issues). Docker sandbox, log capture, pass@1 scoring.
description: SWE-bench Verified is the gold standard for code-agent eval. Real GitHub issues, real PRs as ground truth, real test suites that must pass.
when_to_use: You're building a coding agent (Claude Code / Cursor / Devin-shaped). SWE-bench Verified is the most credible eval - if your agent scores well there, it's actually fixing real bugs.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) you only need code-completion eval - HumanEval is faster; (2) you can't afford ~$50-200 in API costs per run; (3) you don't have a multi-turn agent.
quick_start: 1) Clone the SWE-bench repo  2) Build Docker images for each repo  3) Run your agent on each instance  4) Submit predictions for scoring.
full_code:
"""SWE-bench Verified runner skeleton."""
import json
from datasets import load_dataset
from your_agent import solve_issue   # your coding agent

dataset = load_dataset("princeton-nlp/SWE-bench_Verified", split="test")

predictions = []
for instance in dataset:
    repo = instance["repo"]
    issue = instance["problem_statement"]
    base_commit = instance["base_commit"]
    instance_id = instance["instance_id"]

    # 1. Clone repo + checkout base commit (your agent's environment setup)
    # 2. Feed agent: issue text + repo files (your agent decides what to read)
    # 3. Agent emits a diff (patch) to apply
    patch = solve_issue(issue=issue, repo_path=f"/repos/{repo}", base_commit=base_commit)

    predictions.append({"instance_id": instance_id, "model_name_or_path": "ossai-agent-v1", "model_patch": patch})

with open("predictions.jsonl", "w") as f:
    for p in predictions:
        f.write(json.dumps(p) + "\n")

# Then run the official evaluator (separate process):
# python -m swebench.harness.run_evaluation \
#   --dataset_name princeton-nlp/SWE-bench_Verified \
#   --predictions_path predictions.jsonl \
#   --max_workers 4 \
#   --run_id ossai-v1

for inst in ds:
    patch = my_agent.solve(inst['problem_statement'], inst['repo'], inst['base_commit'])
    predictions.append({'instance_id': inst['instance_id'], 'model_name_or_path': 'ossai-v1', 'model_patch': patch})

with open('predictions.jsonl', 'w') as f:
    for p in predictions:
        f.write(json.dumps(p) + '\n')

Run your agent on each instance, save patches to predictions.jsonl.

title: IFEval Instruction-Following Benchmark (Programmatic, No Judge)
tldr: IFEval suite: checks 25 instruction types like 'use exactly 3 bullet points'. Programmatic verification - no LLM judge needed.
description: IFEval has 541 prompts with verifiable instructions. Each output is checked programmatically (regex, parsing) - no judge variance, no judging cost.
when_to_use: You want to measure if your model follows specific formatting/content instructions (word count, bullets, case, language) - the kind of stuff LLMs routinely fluff. IFEval is deterministic + cheap + signals if your fine-tune kept instruction-following intact.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) you care about response quality not format compliance; (2) your domain has no formatting constraints; (3) you've already passed it with high scores.
quick_start: 1) `pip install ifeval datasets`  2) Generate completions on the IFEval prompts  3) Score with the official verifier.
full_code:
"""IFEval evaluation."""
import json
from datasets import load_dataset
from ifeval import evaluation
import httpx


# 1. Load the 541 IFEval prompts
ds = load_dataset("google/IFEval", split="train")

# 2. Generate completions from your model
completions = []
for ex in ds:
    r = httpx.post(
        "https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages",
        json={"model": "claude-sonnet-4-5", "max_tokens": 1024, "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": ex["prompt"]}]},
        headers={"x-api-key": "...", "anthropic-version": "2023-06-01"},
        timeout=120,
    )
    completions.append({"prompt": ex["prompt"], "response": r.json()["content"][0]["text"], "key": ex["key"]})

# 3. Evaluate
strict_results = evaluation.evaluate_strict(completions, ds)
loose_results = evaluation.evaluate_loose(completions, ds)

print(f"Strict accuracy: {strict_results['accuracy']:.3f}")
print(f"Loose accuracy:  {loose_results['accuracy']:.3f}")
print(f"Per-instruction: {strict_results['per_instruction_acc']}")

response = response.strip()

production_checklist: Run BOTH strict + loose - strict shows your absolute compliance; loose shows your near-misses.
Track per-instruction accuracy - the aggregate hides specific weaknesses.
Run in CI before every model release - instruction-following degrades silently after SFT.
Sample new instructions over time - IFEval is fixed and your training may overfit.
Always use temperature=0 for IFEval - non-zero adds variance, biases the score.
Pair with absolute-quality eval (Arena-Hard, IFBench) - high IFEval + low Arena-Hard means the model is robotic.

faq.question: Why is IFEval popular if it only checks format?
faq.answer: Because format compliance correlates with overall instruction-following capability + it's deterministic + cheap. Models that fail IFEval typically fail nuanced instructions too. It's a fast, free regression signal.

faq.question: Is IFEval contaminated?
faq.answer: Less than HumanEval (it's natural-language-only with verifier semantics that aren't easy to memorize). Still public though - newer fine-tunes likely train on it indirectly. For uncontaminated evals look at IFBench (the successor) or build domain IFEval.

title: Corrective RAG (CRAG) with Web Fallback
tldr: CRAG pipeline: grade retrieved docs, if grade is poor rewrite query and search the web, combine before answering.
description: Self-correcting RAG: LLM grades retrieved chunks; on poor relevance scores, falls back to web search (Tavily) and merges before final answer.
when_to_use: Your retrieval sometimes misses (long-tail queries, ambiguous phrasing). CRAG self-detects via LLM grading + falls back to web search. Catches the 20% of queries vector search alone misses.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) corpus covers all queries; (2) you can't tolerate web search latency (~2s extra); (3) regulated env where web fallback isn't allowed.
quick_start: See repo for full setup steps.
production_checklist: Always eval new RAG technique against baseline on YOUR queries.
Track latency + cost vs quality delta.
Pin library versions for reproducibility.
Cache LLM-side preprocessing (e.g. contextual summaries) - it's expensive.

faq.question: CRAG vs Self-RAG?
faq.answer: CRAG adds web fallback when local retrieval is bad. Self-RAG decides if retrieval is needed at all. Combine them for the most robust RAG.

faq.question: How much does the LLM grader cost?
faq.answer: 1 extra LLM call per query. At Claude Sonnet rates ~$0.001 per call. Cheap insurance vs hallucination cost.

title: Parent-Child Chunking RAG (Precision + Context)
tldr: Embed small child chunks for precise retrieval; return larger parent chunks to the LLM for context. Best precision-recall balance.
description: Hierarchical chunking: index small 256-token chunks for precise vector match, return their 1024-token parent chunks to the LLM for full context.
when_to_use: Your docs have a natural hierarchy (sections, subsections). Parent-child gives the best precision-recall in RAG benchmarks: small chunks match precisely, big chunks give the LLM enough context to answer.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) docs are flat (chat logs, tweets) - hierarchy is forced; (2) very short docs - the parent-child distinction collapses; (3) cost-sensitive - stores 2-3x the chunks.
quick_start: See repo for full setup steps.
code:
from llama_index.core.node_parser import HierarchicalNodeParser
from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex
from llama_index.core.retrievers import AutoMergingRetriever
from llama_index.core.storage import StorageContext

parser = HierarchicalNodeParser.from_defaults(chunk_sizes=[2048, 512, 128])
nodes = parser.get_nodes_from_documents(docs)

storage = StorageContext.from_defaults()
storage.docstore.add_documents(nodes)
leaf_nodes = [n for n in nodes if not n.child_nodes]
index = VectorStoreIndex(leaf_nodes, storage_context=storage)

base_retriever = index.as_retriever(similarity_top_k=12)
retriever = AutoMergingRetriever(base_retriever, storage_context=storage)
# Returns parent nodes when most children match - precision + context
production_checklist: Always eval new RAG technique against baseline on YOUR queries.
Track latency + cost vs quality delta.
Pin library versions for reproducibility.
Cache LLM-side preprocessing (e.g. contextual summaries) - it's expensive.

faq.question: Parent-child vs late chunking?
faq.answer: Parent-child: pre-defined hierarchy, simpler. Late chunking: embed whole doc, chunk after - preserves cross-chunk context naturally. Both improve retrieval quality 5-15% over naive chunking.

faq.question: What sizes work best?
faq.answer: child=128-256 tokens, parent=512-1024. Tune to your domain - legal docs benefit from bigger parents.

title: Anthropic Contextual Retrieval (49% Retrieval Failure Reduction)
tldr: Prepend each chunk with a 50-100 token Claude-generated context summary before embedding. Anthropic's reproducible technique.
description: For each chunk, ask Claude to write a 50-100 token contextual summary explaining its place in the doc. Prepend to chunk before embedding. 49% retrieval failure reduction on Anthropic's benchmarks.
when_to_use: Your chunks lack context when retrieved standalone (e.g. 'The company's Q3 revenue was $X' - which company? which year?). Contextual retrieval prepends the missing context. Most impactful on chunks taken from long structured docs.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) chunks are self-contained; (2) latency-sensitive ingestion - this adds an LLM call per chunk; (3) tiny corpus where the cost outweighs benefit.
quick_start: See repo for full setup steps.
code:
from anthropic import Anthropic
client = Anthropic()

CTX_PROMPT = '''Here is the document:\n<document>{doc}</document>\nHere is a chunk from it:\n<chunk>{chunk}</chunk>\nPlease give a short succinct context to situate this chunk within the overall document. Answer only with the succinct context.'''

def contextualize(doc: str, chunk: str) -> str:
    response = client.messages.create(model='claude-haiku-4-5', max_tokens=200,
        messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': CTX_PROMPT.format(doc=doc, chunk=chunk)}],
        extra_headers={'anthropic-beta': 'prompt-caching-2024-07-31'})
    return f'{response.content[0].text}\n\n{chunk}'

# Apply before embedding:
enhanced_chunks = [contextualize(doc, c) for c in chunks_of(doc)]
production_checklist: Always eval new RAG technique against baseline on YOUR queries.
Track latency + cost vs quality delta.
Pin library versions for reproducibility.
Cache LLM-side preprocessing (e.g. contextual summaries) - it's expensive.

faq.question: Cost of contextualizing 100k chunks?
faq.answer: With prompt caching: ~$5-15 (Claude Haiku) for the full doc context shared across chunks. Without caching: ~10x more. Always use caching for this technique.

faq.question: Use Haiku or Sonnet for contextualization?
faq.answer: Haiku 4.5 is fine - this task is straightforward summarization. Saves 10x cost vs Sonnet for similar quality.

title: HyDE: Hypothetical Document Embeddings
tldr: Generate a hypothetical answer with the LLM, embed THAT, then retrieve real docs near it. Higher recall on abstract queries.
description: HyDE bridges the query-document gap: user query is short + abstract, docs are long + concrete. LLM generates a hypothetical document that 'looks like' the answer; embed THAT for retrieval.
when_to_use: Your queries are short or abstract ('how does X relate to Y?') and your docs are long technical content. HyDE generates a doc-shaped query, which embeds closer to the real docs. Particularly strong on academic/scientific corpora.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) queries already long + concrete; (2) latency-sensitive (adds 1 LLM call); (3) FAQ-shaped traffic where exact query matching is fine.
quick_start: See repo for full setup steps.
code:
from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic
from langchain_qdrant import QdrantVectorStore

llm = ChatAnthropic(model='claude-sonnet-4-5')

HYDE_PROMPT = '''Please write a passage to answer the question.\nQuestion: {q}\nPassage:'''

def hyde_search(question: str, k: int = 5):
    # 1. Generate hypothetical doc
    hypo = llm.invoke(HYDE_PROMPT.format(q=question)).content
    # 2. Embed the hypothetical doc + retrieve real docs
    return store.similarity_search(hypo, k=k)
production_checklist: Always eval new RAG technique against baseline on YOUR queries.
Track latency + cost vs quality delta.
Pin library versions for reproducibility.
Cache LLM-side preprocessing (e.g. contextual summaries) - it's expensive.

faq.question: HyDE vs query rewriting?
faq.answer: Query rewriting: rephrase the QUERY. HyDE: generate the ANSWER and embed that. HyDE works better on abstract questions because the generated passage matches doc shape; query rewriting better for typos / ambiguous phrasing.

faq.question: Does it work with poor LLMs?
faq.answer: HyDE needs a model capable of writing plausible domain passages. Use the same/stronger model as your generator - on Claude Sonnet 4.5+ it's effective; on small open models it can hurt recall.

attribution: OSS AI Hub - original work, MIT-licensed.
title: Multi-Query Retrieval with RRF Fusion
tldr: Generate 3-5 query variations with the LLM, retrieve for each, fuse results via RRF. Reduces single-phrasing dependency.
description: LLM rephrases the user's question 3-5 different ways (different angles, vocabulary, specificity). Retrieve for each, fuse with Reciprocal Rank Fusion. ~10-20% recall gain on ambiguous queries.
when_to_use: User queries vary in phrasing (chat agents, search). Multi-query covers vocabulary mismatch + ambiguity. Combine with hybrid search + reranker for the best recall in 2026 RAG.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) queries always look the same (programmatic API); (2) every query already retrieves perfectly; (3) latency budget tight - adds 1 LLM call + 5 retrievals.
quick_start: See repo for full setup steps.
code:
from collections import defaultdict
from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic

llm = ChatAnthropic(model='claude-sonnet-4-5')

REWRITE_PROMPT = '''Rewrite the user's question in 5 different ways to maximize retrieval recall. Output one per line.\nQuestion: {q}'''

def multi_query_search(question: str, k: int = 8):
    rewrites = llm.invoke(REWRITE_PROMPT.format(q=question)).content.split('\n')
    queries = [question] + [r.strip() for r in rewrites if r.strip()]

    # RRF fusion
    fused = defaultdict(float)
    docs_by_id = {}
    for q in queries:
        hits = store.similarity_search(q, k=20)
        for rank, doc in enumerate(hits):
            doc_id = doc.metadata.get('id', doc.page_content[:50])
            fused[doc_id] += 1 / (60 + rank + 1)
            docs_by_id[doc_id] = doc
    ranked = sorted(fused.items(), key=lambda kv: kv[1], reverse=True)[:k]
    return [docs_by_id[did] for did, _ in ranked]
production_checklist: Always eval new RAG technique against baseline on YOUR queries.
Track latency + cost vs quality delta.
Pin library versions for reproducibility.
Cache LLM-side preprocessing (e.g. contextual summaries) - it's expensive.

faq.question: How many rewrites is optimal?
faq.answer: 3-5 - beyond that recall plateaus. RRF with k=60 fuses cleanly across that range. More than 7 rewrites starts to add noise.

faq.question: Skip the rewrite for short queries?
faq.answer: Yes - if the query is already >15 tokens it's probably specific enough. Add a length-based bypass: `if len(query.split()) > 15: return store.similarity_search(query, k)`.

from anthropic import Anthropic
client = Anthropic()

LONG_SYSTEM = '... 10k token system prompt with tools + instructions ...'  # >=1024 tokens needed

resp = client.messages.create(
    model='claude-sonnet-4-5',

    system=[{'type': 'text', 'text': LONG_SYSTEM, 'cache_control': {'type': 'ephemeral'}}],

)

attribution: OSS AI Hub - original work, MIT-licensed.
title: Bedrock Cohere Rerank 3 for RAG Quality
tldr: Bedrock Cohere Rerank 3 client to re-rank top-k retrieved docs before sending to the LLM. 15-30% RAG quality boost.
description: Bedrock Cohere Rerank 3 client to re-rank top-k retrieved docs before sending to the LLM. 15-30% RAG quality boost.
when_to_use: Your RAG retrieves 20+ chunks but the top-5 isn't always the most relevant. Cohere Rerank 3 re-orders by deep relevance scoring.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) <10 chunks retrieved; (2) reranking adds unacceptable latency; (3) you're on Cohere directly (skip AWS).
quick_start: See repo for full setup steps.
code:
import boto3, json

br = boto3.client('bedrock-agent-runtime', region_name='us-east-1')

def rerank(query: str, docs: list[str], top_n: int = 5):
    res = br.rerank(
        queries=[{'type': 'TEXT', 'textQuery': {'text': query}}],
        sources=[{'type':'INLINE','inlineDocumentSource':{'type':'TEXT','textDocument':{'text': d}}} for d in docs],
        rerankingConfiguration={'type':'BEDROCK_RERANKING_MODEL','bedrockRerankingConfiguration':{'modelConfiguration':{'modelArn':'arn:aws:bedrock:us-east-1::foundation-model/cohere.rerank-v3-5:0'},'numberOfResults':top_n}},
    )
    return [docs[r['index']] for r in res['results']]

title: vLLM Disaggregated Prefill/Decode (Two GPU Pools)
tldr: Disaggregated serving: separate GPU pool for prefill (compute-bound) vs decode (memory-bound). 2-3x throughput at high concurrency.
description: Disaggregated serving: separate GPU pool for prefill (compute-bound) vs decode (memory-bound). 2-3x throughput at high concurrency.
when_to_use: Production at >100 concurrent users with very long inputs. Prefill is FLOPs-bound, decode is memory-bandwidth-bound - separating them avoids resource contention.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) low concurrency; (2) short inputs (prefill not bottleneck); (3) don't have 2 GPU pools.
quick_start: See repo for full setup steps.
code:
# Launch prefill pool
# python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
#   --model meta-llama/Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct \
#   --tensor-parallel-size 4 \
#   --port 8001 \
#   --kv-transfer-config '{"kv_connector":"PyNcclConnector","kv_role":"kv_producer"}'
#
# Launch decode pool
# python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
#   --port 8002 \
#   --kv-transfer-config '{"kv_connector":"PyNcclConnector","kv_role":"kv_consumer"}'

attribution: OSS AI Hub - original work, MIT-licensed.
title: Late Chunking with Jina Embeddings v3
tldr: Embed the whole document then chunk the token-level outputs. Preserves cross-sentence context for retrieval. Long-context embedders.
description: Embed the whole document then chunk the token-level outputs. Preserves cross-sentence context for retrieval. Long-context embedders.
when_to_use: Long docs where cross-section context matters. Late chunking embeds the full doc first - small chunks inherit cross-chunk context naturally.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) short docs; (2) embedder doesn't support long context (BGE-M3, Jina v3, voyage-3 do); (3) cost-sensitive (embedding cost ~5x).
quick_start: See repo for full setup steps.
code:
from transformers import AutoModel, AutoTokenizer
import torch

model = AutoModel.from_pretrained('jinaai/jina-embeddings-v3', trust_remote_code=True)
tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained('jinaai/jina-embeddings-v3')

def late_chunk(text, chunk_tokens=128):
    # Tokenize full doc + embed all token positions
    inputs = tok(text, return_tensors='pt', truncation=True, max_length=8192)
    with torch.no_grad():
        token_embeddings = model(**inputs)[0][0]  # [seq_len, dim]
    # Pool over chunk windows
    chunks = []
    for i in range(0, len(token_embeddings), chunk_tokens):
        chunk_emb = token_embeddings[i:i+chunk_tokens].mean(dim=0)
        chunks.append(chunk_emb.numpy())
    return chunks

Eval against baseline before rolling out.
Track latency + cost.

full_code:
from transformers import AutoModel, AutoTokenizer
import torch

model = AutoModel.from_pretrained('jinaai/jina-embeddings-v3', trust_remote_code=True)
tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained('jinaai/jina-embeddings-v3')

def late_chunk(text, chunk_tokens=128):
    # Tokenize full doc + embed all token positions
    inputs = tok(text, return_tensors='pt', truncation=True, max_length=8192)
    with torch.no_grad():
        token_embeddings = model(**inputs)[0][0]  # [seq_len, dim]
    # Pool over chunk windows
    chunks = []
    for i in range(0, len(token_embeddings), chunk_tokens):
        chunk_emb = token_embeddings[i:i+chunk_tokens].mean(dim=0)
        chunks.append(chunk_emb.numpy())
    return chunks

title: ColBERT Late-Interaction RAG (RAGatouille)
tldr: ColBERTv2 token-level late-interaction retrieval via RAGatouille. Higher accuracy than dense single-vector at 10x storage cost.
description: ColBERTv2 token-level late-interaction retrieval via RAGatouille. Higher accuracy than dense single-vector at 10x storage cost.
when_to_use: When retrieval quality matters more than storage. ColBERT's per-token embeddings + late interaction (MaxSim) beats single-vector dense on BEIR by ~5-10 points.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) storage-constrained (10x size vs single-vector); (2) >10M docs (sharding gets complex); (3) you already nail it with hybrid+rerank.
quick_start: See repo for full setup steps.
code:
from ragatouille import RAGPretrainedModel

rag = RAGPretrainedModel.from_pretrained('colbert-ir/colbertv2.0')
rag.index(
    collection=['doc1 text...', 'doc2 text...', '...'],
    index_name='ossai',
    max_document_length=512,
)
results = rag.search(query='What is the refund policy?', k=5)
for r in results:
    print(r['score'], r['content'][:80])

from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex

)

title: Long-Context RAG with Claude 200K (Skip Chunking)
tldr: Skip chunking entirely: stuff up to 200k tokens of context into Claude. Best for medium corpora (<200k token total).
description: Skip chunking entirely: stuff up to 200k tokens of context into Claude. Best for medium corpora (<200k token total).
when_to_use: Small corpora (<200k tokens, ~500 pages). No chunking + no vector DB. Claude's 200k context just reads everything. Best retrieval quality possible.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) corpus >200k tokens; (2) cost-sensitive ($0.60 per query at full context); (3) latency-sensitive (~2s minimum).
quick_start: See repo for full setup steps.
code:
from anthropic import Anthropic
client = Anthropic()

DOCS = '\n\n'.join([open(p).read() for p in ['policy.md', 'support.md', 'pricing.md']])  # <200k tokens total

def answer(question: str) -> str:
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model='claude-sonnet-4-5', max_tokens=500,
        system=[{'type':'text','text':f'Answer from these docs ONLY:\n\n{DOCS}','cache_control':{'type':'ephemeral'}}],
        messages=[{'role':'user','content':question}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text

title: Semantic Chunker with tiktoken (Boundary-Aware)
tldr: Semantic chunker that respects sentence/paragraph boundaries via tiktoken + sentence-transformers similarity. Beats fixed-size chunking 5-15%.
description: Semantic chunker that respects sentence/paragraph boundaries via tiktoken + sentence-transformers similarity. Beats fixed-size chunking 5-15%.
when_to_use: You're getting weird retrieval results from naive fixed-size chunking (split mid-sentence, lost context). Semantic chunker splits at low-similarity boundaries between sentences.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) parent-child already solves it; (2) very short docs; (3) latency-sensitive ingestion.
quick_start: See repo for full setup steps.
code:
from langchain_experimental.text_splitter import SemanticChunker
from langchain_huggingface import HuggingFaceEmbeddings

embeddings = HuggingFaceEmbeddings(model_name='BAAI/bge-small-en-v1.5')
chunker = SemanticChunker(
    embeddings,
    breakpoint_threshold_type='percentile',
    breakpoint_threshold_amount=90,
)
chunks = chunker.split_text(open('long_doc.md').read())
print(f'{len(chunks)} semantic chunks')

title: Multimodal RAG with CLIP (Text + Image Retrieval)
tldr: Multimodal RAG: embed images + text into the same CLIP space, retrieve relevant images for text queries.
description: Multimodal RAG: embed images + text into the same CLIP space, retrieve relevant images for text queries.
when_to_use: Docs contain figures/charts that matter. CLIP embeddings work across text+image; one vector index for both modalities.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) text-only corpus; (2) high-precision image retrieval needed (use ColBERT-style fine-grained matching); (3) no GPU for batch embedding.
quick_start: See repo for full setup steps.
code:
import torch
from transformers import CLIPModel, CLIPProcessor
from PIL import Image

model = CLIPModel.from_pretrained('openai/clip-vit-large-patch14')
proc = CLIPProcessor.from_pretrained('openai/clip-vit-large-patch14')

def embed_text(text: str): return model.get_text_features(**proc(text=[text], return_tensors='pt'))[0].detach().numpy()
def embed_image(path: str): return model.get_image_features(**proc(images=[Image.open(path)], return_tensors='pt'))[0].detach().numpy()

# Store image+text embeddings in same vector DB. Query with text, retrieve images.

title: Semantic-Similarity RAG Response Cache
tldr: Cache RAG responses by semantic similarity of the query (cosine on query embeddings). 30-60% cost cut on FAQ-shaped traffic.
description: Cache RAG responses by semantic similarity of the query (cosine on query embeddings). 30-60% cost cut on FAQ-shaped traffic.
when_to_use: Your RAG sees repeat-ish questions ('what's the refund policy?' vs 'how do refunds work?'). Cache full responses by semantic similarity instead of exact match.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) every query unique; (2) freshness matters more than cost; (3) you already have a Redis exact cache + that's enough.
quick_start: See repo for full setup steps.
code:
# See /code/redis-llm-response-cache for the implementation - this is the same pattern
# applied at the RAG layer (cache prompt+response after retrieval, keyed by query embedding).
from hashlib import sha256
import numpy as np

def cached_rag(query: str, threshold: float = 0.93):
    qvec = embed(query)
    for cached_qvec, response in cache.iter():
        sim = qvec @ cached_qvec / (np.linalg.norm(qvec) * np.linalg.norm(cached_qvec))
        if sim >= threshold:
            return response
    # Else: run RAG, store response
    response = full_rag(query)
    cache.put(qvec, response)
    return response

title: Pinecone Serverless Vector (Production Pattern)
tldr: Pinecone serverless index: zero-config managed vector DB. Best DX for production teams that don't want to operate Qdrant/Milvus.
description: Pinecone serverless index: zero-config managed vector DB. Best DX for production teams that don't want to operate Qdrant/Milvus.
when_to_use: You want vector search without operating any infra. Pinecone Serverless scales to billion+ vectors with usage-based pricing. Best for teams that prefer paid infra over ops time.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) self-host policy; (2) cost-sensitive (Qdrant Cloud cheaper); (3) need hybrid search (Pinecone's hybrid is weaker than Qdrant's).
quick_start: See repo for full setup steps.
code:
from pinecone import Pinecone, ServerlessSpec
import os

pc = Pinecone(api_key=os.environ['PINECONE_API_KEY'])
if 'ossai' not in pc.list_indexes().names():
    pc.create_index(name='ossai', dimension=384, metric='cosine',
        spec=ServerlessSpec(cloud='aws', region='us-east-1'))
index = pc.Index('ossai')
index.upsert(vectors=[('doc1', [0.1]*384, {'text': 'hello'})])
results = index.query(vector=[0.1]*384, top_k=5, include_metadata=True)

code:
from redis import Redis
from redis.commands.search.field import VectorField, TextField
from redis.commands.search.indexDefinition import IndexDefinition
import numpy as np

r = Redis()
r.ft('idx:docs').create_index([
    TextField('text'),
    VectorField('vec', 'HNSW', {'TYPE': 'FLOAT32', 'DIM': 384, 'DISTANCE_METRIC': 'COSINE'}),
], definition=IndexDefinition(prefix=['doc:']))

r.hset('doc:1', mapping={'text': 'hi', 'vec': np.random.rand(384).astype(np.float32).tobytes()})

attribution: OSS AI Hub - original work, MIT-licensed.
title: Vector DB Migration Tool (Qdrant <-> Pinecone <-> pgvector)
tldr: Migrate vectors + payloads between Qdrant, Pinecone, pgvector, Chroma. Idempotent batching + checksum verification.
description: Migrate vectors + payloads between Qdrant, Pinecone, pgvector, Chroma. Idempotent batching + checksum verification.
when_to_use: You're switching vector DBs (cost, scale, or vendor reasons). This pattern reads in batches from source, writes to destination, verifies counts + sample similarity.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) <100k vectors (manual re-ingest is faster); (2) embedding model changing (must re-embed not migrate); (3) one-time migration done.
quick_start: See repo for full setup steps.
code:
from qdrant_client import QdrantClient
import pinecone

src = QdrantClient('http://qdrant:6333')
dst_pc = pinecone.Pinecone().Index('ossai')

offset = None
while True:
    points, offset = src.scroll(collection_name='ossai', limit=100, offset=offset, with_vectors=True, with_payload=True)
    if not points: break
    dst_pc.upsert(vectors=[(p.id, p.vector, p.payload) for p in points])
    if offset is None: break
print('migration complete')

when_to_use: You have labeled data and want prompts to optimize automatically vs hand-tuning. DSPy compiles prompts via gradient-free optimization - effectively 'prompt fine-tuning'.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) no labeled data; (2) prefer explicit prompts; (3) team unfamiliar with DSPy's signature/module abstractions.
quick_start: See repo for full setup steps.
code:
import dspy
from dspy.teleprompt import BootstrapFewShot

lm = dspy.LM('anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-5')
dspy.settings.configure(lm=lm)

class QA(dspy.Signature):
    'Answer questions from context.'
    context = dspy.InputField()
    question = dspy.InputField()
    answer = dspy.OutputField()

cot = dspy.ChainOfThought(QA)
# trainset = [dspy.Example(context='...', question='...', answer='...').with_inputs('context','question') for _ in range(20)]
optimizer = BootstrapFewShot(metric=lambda ex, pred, _: ex.answer.lower() in pred.answer.lower())
optimized_cot = optimizer.compile(cot, trainset=trainset)

attribution: OSS AI Hub - original work, MIT-licensed.
title: LangGraph Self-Correcting Coder (Code -> Test -> Fix Loop)
tldr: Self-correcting code agent: generate code, run it, capture errors, regenerate with error context. Until tests pass or max iter.
description: Self-correcting code agent: generate code, run it, capture errors, regenerate with error context. Until tests pass or max iter.
when_to_use: Code generation tasks where 'first attempt is right' fails often. Self-correcting loop adds 30-50% pass-rate at the cost of 2-5x latency.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) trivial generation tasks; (2) latency-critical; (3) no sandboxed code execution available.
quick_start: See repo for full setup steps.
code:
from typing import TypedDict
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END

class State(TypedDict):
    task: str
    code: str
    error: str
    iteration: int

def generate(s): return {'code': llm_generate(s['task'], error=s.get('error',''))}
def run(s):
    try: exec(s['code']); return {'error': ''}
    except Exception as e: return {'error': str(e), 'iteration': s.get('iteration', 0)+1}
def route(s): return 'done' if not s['error'] or s['iteration'] > 5 else 'generate'

g = StateGraph(State)
g.add_node('generate', generate); g.add_node('run', run)
g.set_entry_point('generate')
g.add_edge('generate', 'run')
g.add_conditional_edges('run', route, {'generate': 'generate', 'done': END})
production_checklist: Pin library versions.
Cap iterations + timeouts.
Log every step to Langfuse/LangSmith.
Test failure paths in CI.

from typing import TypedDict
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END

class State(TypedDict):
    task: str
    code: str
    error: str
    iteration: int

def generate(s): return {'code': llm_generate(s['task'], error=s.get('error',''))}
def run(s):
    try: exec(s['code']); return {'error': ''}
    except Exception as e: return {'error': str(e), 'iteration': s.get('iteration', 0)+1}
def route(s): return 'done' if not s['error'] or s['iteration'] > 5 else 'generate'

g = StateGraph(State)
g.add_node('generate', generate); g.add_node('run', run)
g.set_entry_point('generate')
g.add_edge('generate', 'run')
g.add_conditional_edges('run', route, {'generate': 'generate', 'done': END})

title: BabyAGI Task-List Autonomous Agent
tldr: Classic BabyAGI: maintains a task list, executes the top task, creates new tasks from results, prioritizes, repeats.
description: Classic BabyAGI: maintains a task list, executes the top task, creates new tasks from results, prioritizes, repeats.
when_to_use: Educational + showing the basic 'autonomous agent' pattern. Open-ended exploration tasks where 'what to do next?' is part of the problem.
when_not_to_use: Skip for any real production - BabyAGI is a research pattern, not a production framework. Modern agents (LangGraph, smolagents) are more robust.
quick_start: See repo for full setup steps.
code:
import openai
from collections import deque

tasks = deque(['Research vLLM 0.7 features'])
objective = 'Write a 200-word brief on vLLM 0.7.'
results = []
while tasks and len(results) < 5:
    task = tasks.popleft()
    out = openai.chat.completions.create(model='gpt-5', messages=[{'role':'user','content':f'Do: {task}'}]).choices[0].message.content
    results.append((task, out))
    new_tasks = openai.chat.completions.create(model='gpt-5', messages=[
        {'role':'user','content':f'Given objective: {objective}\nWe just did: {task}\nResult: {out}\nList 1-3 next tasks.'}
    ]).choices[0].message.content.split('\n')
    tasks.extend(t.strip('- ') for t in new_tasks if t.strip())

import openai
from collections import deque

tasks = deque(['Research vLLM 0.7 features'])
objective = 'Write a 200-word brief on vLLM 0.7.'
results = []
while tasks and len(results) < 5:
    task = tasks.popleft()
    out = openai.chat.completions.create(model='gpt-5', messages=[{'role':'user','content':f'Do: {task}'}]).choices[0].message.content
    results.append((task, out))
    new_tasks = openai.chat.completions.create(model='gpt-5', messages=[
        {'role':'user','content':f'Given objective: {objective}\nWe just did: {task}\nResult: {out}\nList 1-3 next tasks.'}
    ]).choices[0].message.content.split('\n')
    tasks.extend(t.strip('- ') for t in new_tasks if t.strip())

from a2a import AgentCard, A2AServer, Task

    name='ossai-research-agent',

    skills=[{'name': 'web_search'}, {'name': 'summarize'}],

)

    yield {'type': 'result', 'data': '...'}

server = A2AServer(agent_card=card, task_handler=task_handler)

title: MCP Gmail Server (with PII Redaction)
tldr: MCP server for Gmail: OAuth2, label management, send/draft, built-in PII redaction on outgoing messages.
description: MCP server for Gmail: OAuth2, label management, send/draft, built-in PII redaction on outgoing messages.
when_to_use: Agents that triage email / draft replies / file emails. PII redaction is critical when LLMs are reading/writing email content.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) inbound email only (use a different transport); (2) sensitive industry without explicit policy approval; (3) Gmail isn't your stack.
quick_start: See repo for full setup steps.
code:
from mcp.server.fastmcp import FastMCP
from googleapiclient.discovery import build
from google.oauth2.credentials import Credentials
import os, re

mcp = FastMCP('gmail')
creds = Credentials.from_authorized_user_file('token.json')
svc = build('gmail', 'v1', credentials=creds)

def redact(text):
    text = re.sub(r'\b\d{4}-?\d{4}-?\d{4}-?\d{4}\b', '[CARD]', text)
    text = re.sub(r'\b\d{3}-\d{2}-\d{4}\b', '[SSN]', text)
    return text

@mcp.tool()
async def search(q: str) -> list:
    res = svc.users().messages().list(userId='me', q=q, maxResults=10).execute()
    return res.get('messages', [])

if __name__ == '__main__': mcp.run()
production_checklist: Pin library versions.
Cap iterations + timeouts.
Log every step to Langfuse/LangSmith.
Test failure paths in CI.

from mcp.server.fastmcp import FastMCP
from googleapiclient.discovery import build
from google.oauth2.credentials import Credentials
import os, re

mcp = FastMCP('gmail')
creds = Credentials.from_authorized_user_file('token.json')
svc = build('gmail', 'v1', credentials=creds)

def redact(text):
    text = re.sub(r'\b\d{4}-?\d{4}-?\d{4}-?\d{4}\b', '[CARD]', text)
    text = re.sub(r'\b\d{3}-\d{2}-\d{4}\b', '[SSN]', text)
    return text

@mcp.tool()
async def search(q: str) -> list:
    res = svc.users().messages().list(userId='me', q=q, maxResults=10).execute()
    return res.get('messages', [])

if __name__ == '__main__': mcp.run()

title: MCP Test Harness (Verify Servers Conform to Spec)
tldr: Test harness that exercises an MCP server: list_tools / call_tool / error responses. Catches spec drift early.
description: Test harness that exercises an MCP server: list_tools / call_tool / error responses. Catches spec drift early.
when_to_use: Maintaining a custom MCP server. The harness gives you reproducible tests against the spec - catches breaking changes when you upgrade the SDK.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) you're not maintaining an MCP server; (2) using official servers (they have their own tests); (3) one-off prototype.
quick_start: See repo for full setup steps.
code:
import asyncio
from mcp.client.session import ClientSession
from mcp.client.stdio import StdioServerParameters, stdio_client

async def test_server():
    params = StdioServerParameters(command='python', args=['my_server.py'])
    async with stdio_client(params) as (read, write):
        async with ClientSession(read, write) as session:
            await session.initialize()
            tools = await session.list_tools()
            assert 'echo' in [t.name for t in tools.tools]
            result = await session.call_tool('echo', {'text': 'hi'})
            assert 'Echoed: hi' in result.content[0].text
            print('PASS')

asyncio.run(test_server())

attribution: OSS AI Hub - original work, MIT-licensed.
title: Structured Logging with Request-ID Propagation
tldr: structlog setup with auto-propagated request_id + user_id context. Pipes to JSON for Loki / Splunk / Datadog ingestion.
description: structlog setup with auto-propagated request_id + user_id context. Pipes to JSON for Loki / Splunk / Datadog ingestion.
when_to_use: Any production LLM service. Structured JSON logs let you slice by user/tenant/error type in your log aggregator; request_id ties all log lines across services.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) prototype only; (2) you're full-OTel + don't want both; (3) deep custom logging system already.
quick_start: See repo for full setup steps.
code:
import structlog
from contextvars import ContextVar

request_id_ctx = ContextVar('request_id', default='-')

structlog.configure(
    processors=[
        structlog.contextvars.merge_contextvars,
        structlog.processors.add_log_level,
        structlog.processors.TimeStamper(fmt='iso'),
        structlog.processors.JSONRenderer(),
    ],
)
log = structlog.get_logger()

# In middleware:
import uuid
async def middleware(request, call_next):
    rid = request.headers.get('x-request-id') or str(uuid.uuid4())
    structlog.contextvars.bind_contextvars(request_id=rid, user_id=request.user_id)
    return await call_next(request)

log.info('llm.call', model='claude-sonnet-4-5', tokens_in=100, tokens_out=250)
production_checklist: Pin library versions.
Add retries + circuit breaker.
Log + monitor metrics.
Smoke test in CI.

attribution: OSS AI Hub - original work, MIT-licensed.
title: LLM-Routed Tool Selection (Two-Stage)
tldr: Two-stage pattern: small/cheap LLM picks the right 5 tools from your 50, capable model uses only those. Reduces hallucinated tool calls.
description: Two-stage pattern: small/cheap LLM picks the right 5 tools from your 50, capable model uses only those. Reduces hallucinated tool calls.
when_to_use: You have 30+ tools but Claude/GPT degrades when you stuff them all into one prompt. Router picks the relevant subset (5-10 tools) before the main call.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) you have <15 tools; (2) router overhead > savings; (3) tools are deeply context-dependent (router can't know without context).
quick_start: See repo for full setup steps.
code:
from anthropic import Anthropic
client = Anthropic()

ALL_TOOLS = {...}  # 50 tool descriptions keyed by name

def select_tools(question: str, k: int = 5) -> list[dict]:
    descs = '\n'.join(f'- {n}: {t["description"]}' for n, t in ALL_TOOLS.items())
    out = client.messages.create(model='claude-haiku-4-5', max_tokens=200,
        messages=[{'role':'user','content':f'Pick the {k} most relevant tools for:\n{question}\n\nTools:\n{descs}\n\nReply with names, one per line.'}],
    ).content[0].text
    names = [n.strip() for n in out.split('\n') if n.strip() in ALL_TOOLS]
    return [ALL_TOOLS[n] for n in names[:k]]
production_checklist: Pin library versions.
Add retries + circuit breaker.
Log + monitor metrics.
Smoke test in CI.

from anthropic import Anthropic
client = Anthropic()

ALL_TOOLS = {...}  # 50 tool descriptions keyed by name

def select_tools(question: str, k: int = 5) -> list[dict]:
    descs = '\n'.join(f'- {n}: {t["description"]}' for n, t in ALL_TOOLS.items())
    out = client.messages.create(model='claude-haiku-4-5', max_tokens=200,
        messages=[{'role':'user','content':f'Pick the {k} most relevant tools for:\n{question}\n\nTools:\n{descs}\n\nReply with names, one per line.'}],
    ).content[0].text
    names = [n.strip() for n in out.split('\n') if n.strip() in ALL_TOOLS]
    return [ALL_TOOLS[n] for n in names[:k]]

title: Guardrails-AI Structured Output Validator
tldr: Guardrails-AI RAIL spec for output validation with auto-reasking on failure. PII detection + toxic-output blocking.
description: Guardrails-AI RAIL spec for output validation with auto-reasking on failure. PII detection + toxic-output blocking.
when_to_use: You need richer validation than Pydantic - PII detection, toxic content, prompt-injection. Guardrails has built-in validators for these classes.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) Pydantic + Instructor is enough; (2) you've outgrown Guardrails (the framework moves slow); (3) homemade validators are simpler.
quick_start: See repo for full setup steps.
code:
from guardrails import Guard
from guardrails.hub import ToxicLanguage, DetectPII

guard = Guard().use_many(
    DetectPII(['PHONE_NUMBER', 'EMAIL_ADDRESS'], on_fail='filter'),
    ToxicLanguage(threshold=0.5, on_fail='exception'),
)
result = guard(
    llm_api=anthropic_client.messages.create,
    model='claude-sonnet-4-5',
    messages=[{'role':'user','content':'Tell me about LLMs.'}],
)
print(result.validated_output)

title: ARQ Async Task Queue (Redis-Native Asyncio)
tldr: ARQ task queue: built on asyncio + Redis Streams. Lighter than Celery, fully async-native.
description: ARQ task queue: built on asyncio + Redis Streams. Lighter than Celery, fully async-native.
when_to_use: Modern Python (asyncio-first) where you want simpler-than-Celery. ARQ embraces async/await + Redis Streams natively.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) Celery's ecosystem features (beat, flower, signals) matter; (2) sync-first codebase; (3) team already on Celery.
quick_start: See repo for full setup steps.
code:
from arq import create_pool
from arq.connections import RedisSettings
from anthropic import AsyncAnthropic

claude = AsyncAnthropic()

async def llm_task(ctx, prompt):
    msg = await claude.messages.create(model='claude-sonnet-4-5', max_tokens=500,
        messages=[{'role':'user','content':prompt}])
    return msg.content[0].text

class WorkerSettings:
    functions = [llm_task]
    redis_settings = RedisSettings(host='localhost', port=6379)

# Run: arq worker.WorkerSettings
# Enqueue: redis = await create_pool(RedisSettings()); await redis.enqueue_job('llm_task', 'hi')

title: Cost-Tracking Middleware (Per-User Attribution)
tldr: FastAPI middleware that logs every LLM call with model, tokens, computed cost, user_id. Outputs to Postgres or ClickHouse.
description: FastAPI middleware that logs every LLM call with model, tokens, computed cost, user_id. Outputs to Postgres or ClickHouse.
when_to_use: Multi-tenant LLM apps where you need to attribute costs to users / tenants. Middleware captures every call - the only reliable way to bill accurately.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) single-tenant; (2) using Helicone/Langfuse for this; (3) all your traffic goes through OpenRouter (which provides per-key cost).
quick_start: See repo for full setup steps.
code:
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request
import time, asyncpg, json

PRICES = {
    'claude-sonnet-4-5': {'in': 3.0/1e6, 'out': 15.0/1e6},
    'gpt-5':            {'in': 5.0/1e6, 'out': 20.0/1e6},
}

async def log_cost(pool, user_id, model, tokens_in, tokens_out):
    p = PRICES.get(model, {'in':0,'out':0})
    cost = tokens_in * p['in'] + tokens_out * p['out']
    async with pool.acquire() as conn:
        await conn.execute('INSERT INTO llm_costs (user_id, model, tokens_in, tokens_out, cost_usd, ts) VALUES ($1,$2,$3,$4,$5,now())',
            user_id, model, tokens_in, tokens_out, cost)
production_checklist: Pin library versions.
Add retries + circuit breaker.
Log + monitor metrics.
Smoke test in CI.

attribution: OSS AI Hub - original work, MIT-licensed.
title: Graceful Shutdown for In-Flight LLM Requests
tldr: FastAPI lifecycle handler that drains in-flight streaming responses before shutting down. Critical for blue-green deploys.
description: FastAPI lifecycle handler that drains in-flight streaming responses before shutting down. Critical for blue-green deploys.
when_to_use: Blue-green / rolling deploys of streaming LLM endpoints. Without draining, mid-stream responses are dropped and users see truncated text.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) batch-only workloads; (2) you can tolerate truncated streams; (3) Kubernetes preStop hook handles it externally.
quick_start: See repo for full setup steps.
code:
import asyncio, signal
from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
from fastapi import FastAPI

active: set = set()

@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
    yield
    # Drain on shutdown
    deadline = asyncio.get_event_loop().time() + 30
    while active and asyncio.get_event_loop().time() < deadline:
        await asyncio.sleep(0.5)
    print(f'shutdown with {len(active)} active streams remaining')

app = FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan)

async def streaming_endpoint():
    task = asyncio.current_task()
    active.add(task)
    try:
        async for chunk in your_llm_stream():
            yield chunk
    finally:
        active.discard(task)
production_checklist: Pin library versions.
Add retries + circuit breaker.
Log + monitor metrics.
Smoke test in CI.

meta_description: FastAPI lifecycle handler that drains in-flight streaming responses before shutting down. Critical for blue-green deploys.
full_code:
import asyncio, signal
from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
from fastapi import FastAPI

active: set = set()

@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
    yield
    # Drain on shutdown
    deadline = asyncio.get_event_loop().time() + 30
    while active and asyncio.get_event_loop().time() < deadline:
        await asyncio.sleep(0.5)
    print(f'shutdown with {len(active)} active streams remaining')

app = FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan)

async def streaming_endpoint():
    task = asyncio.current_task()
    active.add(task)
    try:
        async for chunk in your_llm_stream():
            yield chunk
    finally:
        active.discard(task)

description: Deploy a custom container model to Vertex AI Endpoint with autoscaling, A/B traffic splitting, online prediction logging.

code:
from google.cloud import aiplatform

aiplatform.init(project='ossai', location='us-central1')

model = aiplatform.Model.upload(
    display_name='llama-3.3-70b-vllm',
    serving_container_image_uri='gcr.io/ossai/vllm:0.7.0',
    serving_container_ports=[8000],
    serving_container_health_route='/health',
    serving_container_predict_route='/v1/chat/completions',
)
endpoint = model.deploy(
    machine_type='a3-highgpu-8g', accelerator_type='NVIDIA_H100_80GB', accelerator_count=4,
    min_replica_count=1, max_replica_count=4,
)

meta_title: Azure AI Foundry Deploy (Managed Online Endpoint) | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: Azure AI Foundry deployment of a fine-tuned model: managed online endpoint, Application Insights, content-filter config.
full_code:
# endpoint.yaml
$schema: https://azuremlschemas.azureedge.net/latest/managedOnlineEndpoint.schema.json
name: ossai-llama
auth_mode: key

# deployment.yaml
$schema: https://azuremlschemas.azureedge.net/latest/managedOnlineDeployment.schema.json
name: blue
endpoint_name: ossai-llama
model: azureml:llama-33-70b:1
instance_type: Standard_ND96isr_H100_v5
instance_count: 1
liveness_probe:
  failure_threshold: 30
  period: 30

# az ml online-endpoint create -f endpoint.yaml
# az ml online-deployment create -f deployment.yaml --all-traffic

title: Fly.io GPU LLM Deploy (Simple, Multi-Region)
tldr: fly.toml deploying an Ollama-based LLM to a Fly.io GPU machine. Volume mount for model cache + global app distribution.
description: fly.toml deploying an Ollama-based LLM to a Fly.io GPU machine. Volume mount for model cache + global app distribution.
when_to_use: Solo dev / small team wanting GPU deploys without K8s. Fly.io GPU machines are simpler than K8s + give you global multi-region for free.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) need autoscaling beyond Fly.io's machine model; (2) sustained heavy traffic (cheaper on Lambda Labs / Crusoe); (3) need Modal-style serverless.
quick_start: See repo for full setup steps.
code:
# fly.toml
app = 'ossai-llama'
primary_region = 'iad'

[build]
  image = 'ollama/ollama:latest'

[mounts]
  source = 'ollama_data'
  destination = '/root/.ollama'

[[vm]]
  size = 'a100-40gb'
  memory = '32gb'

[services]
  internal_port = 11434
  protocol = 'tcp'
  [[services.ports]]
    port = 80
    handlers = ['http']

# fly volumes create ollama_data --size 100 --region iad
# fly deploy
production_checklist: Pin library versions.

Log + monitor metrics.
Smoke test in CI.
